concepts.title: Three Elements of Successful Trading
concepts.body: Murphy identifies three core elements required for successful trading, which are addressed in the Money Management and Trading Tactics chapter. While the full elaboration appears later in the book, the table of contents signals that trading success is multi-dimensional and not reducible to signal generation alone.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Philosophy and Rationale of Technical Analysis
concepts.body: Murphy opens the book by establishing the philosophical foundation of technical analysis — the idea that price action reflects all available information and that studying price charts can yield actionable forecasts. This rationale underpins every technique discussed in the book.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Technical versus Fundamental Forecasting
concepts.body: Murphy distinguishes technical forecasting (based on price and volume data) from fundamental forecasting (based on economic supply/demand factors). The table of contents treats these as distinct but potentially complementary approaches, with technical analysis offering superior timing capability.
related_concepts: Philosophy and Rationale of Technical Analysis
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Analysis versus Timing
concepts.body: Murphy separates the act of market analysis from the act of trade timing, implying that even correct fundamental analysis can fail without precise entry and exit timing — an area where technical analysis excels.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Flexibility and Adaptability of Technical Analysis
concepts.body: Technical analysis is presented as a flexible methodology that can be adapted across different markets and time frames. This adaptability is a core advantage over more market-specific fundamental approaches.
related_concepts: Technical Analysis Applied to Different Trading Mediums
Technical Analysis Applied to Different Time Dimensions
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Technical Analysis Across Markets and Time Frames
concepts.body: Murphy explicitly states that technical analysis can be applied to different trading mediums (stocks, futures, forex, etc.) and different time dimensions (intraday, daily, weekly, monthly). This universality is a foundational claim of the technical approach.
related_concepts: Flexibility and Adaptability of Technical Analysis
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Technical Analysis as Economic Forecasting Tool
concepts.body: Murphy argues that technical analysis of financial markets can serve as a form of economic forecasting, since market prices tend to lead economic conditions. This elevates technical analysis beyond mere trading signal generation.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Technician versus Chartist Distinction
concepts.body: Murphy addresses the distinction between the terms 'technician' and 'chartist,' suggesting that while chartists rely primarily on visual chart patterns, technicians employ a broader toolkit including quantitative indicators. The terms are often used interchangeably but carry different connotations.
related_concepts: Philosophy and Rationale of Technical Analysis
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Technical Analysis in Stocks versus Futures
concepts.body: Murphy provides a brief comparison of how technical analysis differs when applied to stocks versus futures markets, noting that futures analysis relies less on broad market averages and indicators. This distinction is important for practitioners moving between asset classes.
related_concepts: Less Reliance on Market Averages in Futures
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Less Reliance on Market Averages in Futures
concepts.body: Unlike stock market technical analysis, futures technical analysis places less emphasis on broad market averages and sentiment indicators. Each futures market is analyzed more on its own technical merits.
related_concepts: Technical Analysis in Stocks versus Futures
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Criticisms of the Technical Approach
concepts.body: Murphy acknowledges that technical analysis faces criticisms, which he addresses directly. These criticisms represent known limitations or challenges to the technical approach that practitioners should be aware of.
related_concepts: Philosophy and Rationale of Technical Analysis
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Random Walk Theory as Challenge to Technicals
concepts.body: The Random Walk Theory — which holds that price changes are random and therefore unpredictable — is presented as a major academic criticism of technical analysis. Murphy addresses this theory, implying that long-term chart trends dispute the randomness hypothesis.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Universal Principles of Technical Analysis
concepts.body: Murphy introduces the idea that certain technical principles are universal — applicable across all markets and time frames. This universality is a key philosophical claim that justifies the broad application of the techniques described throughout the book.
related_concepts: Flexibility and Adaptability of Technical Analysis
Technical Analysis Across Markets and Time Frames
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Volume and Open Interest as Secondary Indicators
concepts.body: Murphy classifies volume and open interest as secondary indicators — important confirmatory tools but subordinate to price action as the primary signal. They are used to validate or question the strength of price moves.
related_concepts: Interpretation of Volume for All Markets
Interpretation of Open Interest in Futures
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Oscillator Usage in Conjunction with Trend
concepts.body: Murphy establishes that oscillators are most effective when used in conjunction with the prevailing trend rather than in isolation. This is a foundational rule for applying momentum and overbought/oversold indicators.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Principle of Contrary Opinion in Futures
concepts.body: Murphy includes contrary opinion as a tool in futures trading, where extreme bullish or bearish sentiment readings can signal potential market reversals. This is a sentiment-based complement to price-based technical analysis.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Long Term Charts Not for Direct Trading
concepts.body: Murphy explicitly states that long-term charts are not intended for direct trading purposes. They serve to provide perspective on the broader trend environment rather than to generate specific entry or exit signals.
related_concepts: Long Term to Short Term Charts
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Combining Cycle Lengths for Analysis
concepts.body: Murphy discusses combining multiple cycle lengths to improve analytical accuracy. Dominant cycles and their combinations are used to anticipate turning points in markets.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Pros and Cons of System Trading
concepts.body: Murphy addresses the advantages and disadvantages of mechanical trading systems, implying that no system is perfect and that discretionary judgment remains important. This is relevant to the edge-condition awareness of any systematic approach.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Dow Theory Applied to Futures Trading
concepts.body: Murphy extends Dow Theory — originally developed for stock market analysis — to futures trading, suggesting its basic tenets about trend identification and confirmation are broadly applicable across asset classes.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Definition of Technical Analysis
concepts.body: Technical analysis is formally defined by Murphy as the study of market action, primarily through the use of charts, for the purpose of forecasting future price trends. The term 'market action' encompasses the three principal sources of information available to the technician. This definition establishes the scope and purpose of the entire discipline.

quotes.text: Technical analysis is the study of market action, primarily through the use of charts, for the purpose of forecasting future price trends.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Philosophical Basis of Technical Analysis
concepts.body: Murphy emphasizes that a full appreciation of technical analysis must begin with understanding both what it claims to do and the philosophical rationale behind those claims. The approach is grounded in specific premises that distinguish it from other forms of market analysis. Understanding these premises is presented as a prerequisite to studying the tools and techniques.

quotes.text: The author's strong belief is that a full appreciation of the technical approach must begin with a clear understanding of what technical analysis claims to be able to do and, maybe even more importantly, the philosophy or rationale on which it bases those claims.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Technical vs. Fundamental Analysis Distinction
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] Murphy states that one of the goals of the introductory chapter is to draw clear distinctions between technical and fundamental analysis, and to address criticisms frequently raised against the technical approach. This framing positions technical analysis as a distinct discipline with its own philosophical underpinnings separate from fundamental analysis.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Intermarket Analysis as a Branch of Technical Analysis
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] Murphy describes intermarket analysis as a branch of technical analysis he helped create, examining the links between bonds and stocks, bonds and commodities, commodities and the dollar, and stock sectors. It is presented as a broader application of technical principles across all financial markets, including the use of relative strength analysis and top-down market approaches.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Relative Strength Analysis
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] Relative strength analysis is listed as a key component of intermarket analysis, applied to sectors and individual stocks. It is used to compare the performance of one asset against another or against a benchmark, helping identify which sectors or stocks are outperforming or underperforming.
related_concepts: Intermarket Analysis as a Branch of Technical Analysis
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Top-Down Market Approach
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] The top-down market approach is referenced in the context of intermarket analysis, implying a methodology that begins with broad market analysis before narrowing to sectors and individual stocks. It is listed alongside deflation scenario analysis and intermarket correlation as part of the intermarket analytical framework.
related_concepts: Intermarket Analysis as a Branch of Technical Analysis
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Market Breadth Indicators
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] The book covers a range of market breadth indicators including the Advance-Decline Line, McClellan Oscillator, McClellan Summation Index, New High-New Low Index, Upside vs. Downside Volume, and the Arms Index (TRIN). These are used to measure the internal strength or weakness of a market beyond simple price averages.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Building a Trading System: 5-Step Plan
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] The book outlines a 5-step plan for building a trading system: (1) Start with a concept/idea, (2) Turn the idea into a set of objective rules, (3) Visually check it on charts, (4) Formally test it with a computer, and (5) Evaluate results. Money management is also addressed as part of the system-building process.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Money Management in Trading Systems
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] Money management is presented as a distinct component of trading system development, addressed after the 5-step plan for building a system. Topics covered include reward-to-risk ratios, trading multiple positions (trending vs. trading units), position sizing, and guidelines for what to do after periods of success and adversity.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Reward to Risk Ratios
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] Reward-to-risk ratios are listed as a key money management concept, implying that trades should be evaluated based on the potential reward relative to the risk taken. This is part of the broader money management framework discussed in the trading tactics chapter.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Intraday Pivot Points
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] The use of intraday pivot points is listed as a tactical tool for transitioning from daily charts to intraday price charts. Pivot points serve as reference levels for intraday trading decisions.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Continuous Futures Contracts
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] The book addresses the construction of continuous futures contracts, covering several methods: nearest contract, next contract, Gann contract, continuous contracts, and constant forward continuous contracts. These methods are used to create unbroken price histories for futures markets to enable technical analysis across contract expirations.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Market Profile Framework
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] Market Profile is introduced as a method for organizing and interpreting market activity, covering the Market Profile graphic, market structure, organizing principles, range development and profile patterns, and tracking longer-term market activity. It is presented as a distinct analytical framework within technical analysis.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Universality of Technical Principles
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] Murphy states that the principles of technical analysis are universal, making it straightforward to broaden their application from futures markets to all financial markets including stocks. This universality is a foundational claim of the technical approach.

quotes.text: Since the principles of technical analysis are universal, it wasn't that difficult to broaden the focus to include all financial markets.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Market Action Discounts Everything
concepts.body: The cornerstone premise of technical analysis holds that all factors that could possibly affect price — fundamental, political, psychological, or otherwise — are already reflected in the market price. Therefore, studying price action alone is sufficient for forecasting. The technician indirectly studies fundamentals by reading price, since rising prices imply bullish fundamentals and falling prices imply bearish fundamentals.

quotes.text: The technician believes that anything that can possibly affect the price—fundamentally, politically, psychologically, or otherwise—is actually reflected in the price of that market.
market action discounts everything

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Prices Move in Trends
concepts.body: The premise that markets trend is absolutely essential to the technical approach. The entire purpose of charting is to identify trends in their early stages and trade in the direction of those trends. Most technical techniques are trend-following in nature, designed to identify and follow existing trends rather than predict reversals.

quotes.text: The whole purpose of charting the price action of a market is to identify trends in early stages of their development for the purpose of trading in the direction of those trends.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: History Repeats Itself
concepts.body: The third foundational premise of technical analysis is that history repeats itself. This underpins the use of historical chart patterns and technical indicators, as past market behavior is expected to recur due to consistent human psychology in markets.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Three Premises of Technical Analysis
concepts.body: Technical analysis rests on three core premises: (1) market action discounts everything, (2) prices move in trends, and (3) history repeats itself. These premises form the philosophical foundation upon which all technical tools and methods are built. Accepting all three is a prerequisite for the technical approach to be meaningful.

quotes.text: There are three premises on which the technical approach is based: 1. Market action discounts everything. 2. Prices move in trends. 3. History repeats itself.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Market Action vs Price Action Terminology
concepts.body: The author distinguishes 'price action' from 'market action,' noting that most technicians include volume and open interest — not just price — as integral parts of market analysis. 'Price action' is considered too narrow a term. Open interest is relevant only in futures and options markets.

quotes.text: The term 'price action,' which is often used, seems too narrow because most technicians include volume and open interest as an integral part of their market analysis.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Technical Analysis vs Fundamental Analysis
concepts.body: The technician does not concern themselves with the reasons why prices rise or fall, particularly in early trend stages or at critical turning points where the reasons are often unknown. The chartist lets the market indicate direction rather than trying to outsmart or outguess it. Technicians indirectly study fundamentals through price, since price reflects supply and demand forces.

quotes.text: As a rule, chartists do not concern themselves with the reasons why prices rise or fall.
The chartist does not necessarily try to outsmart or outguess the market.
The chartist knows there are reasons why markets go up or down. He or she just doesn't believe that knowing what those reasons are is necessary in the forecasting process.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Supply and Demand as Price Driver
concepts.body: The technical premise that market action discounts everything is grounded in supply and demand economics: if demand exceeds supply, prices rise; if supply exceeds demand, prices fall. The technician reverses this logic — rising prices imply bullish fundamentals (demand exceeding supply) and falling prices imply bearish fundamentals. Charts reflect the bullish or bearish psychology of the marketplace but do not cause market moves.

quotes.text: If demand exceeds supply, prices should rise. If supply exceeds demand, prices should fall.
The charts do not in themselves cause markets to move up or down. They simply reflect the bullish or bearish psychology of the marketplace.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Trend-Following as Core Technical Method
concepts.body: Most technical techniques are trend-following in nature, meaning their intent is to identify and follow existing trends rather than predict turning points. The goal is to identify trends in their early stages and trade in the direction of those trends. Accepting that markets trend is a prerequisite for using these methods.

quotes.text: most of the techniques used in this approach are trend-following in nature, meaning that their intent is to identify and follow existing trends.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Entry Direction Aligned with Trend
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] The author states that the purpose of charting is to identify trends in their early stages for the purpose of trading in the direction of those trends. This implies that entries should be made in the direction of the identified trend, not against it.

quotes.text: The whole purpose of charting the price action of a market is to identify trends in early stages of their development for the purpose of trading in the direction of those trends.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Random Walk Theory Rejection by Technicians
concepts.body: The technical community wholly rejects the idea that markets are random. The author argues that if markets were truly random, no forecasting technique would work, and the very existence of successful technical analysis contradicts the random walk premise.

quotes.text: the idea that markets are random is totally rejected by the technical community. If the markets were truly random, no forecasting technique would work.

related_concepts: Efficient Market Hypothesis vs. Technical Premise
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Efficient Market Hypothesis vs. Technical Premise
concepts.body: The Efficient Market Hypothesis (EMH) holds that markets quickly discount all information, making it impossible to profit from that information. The author notes this is actually very close to the technical premise that markets discount everything — the key difference is that technicians believe price action itself reveals discounted information before it becomes publicly known, making price monitoring valuable.

quotes.text: Far from disproving the validity of the technical approach, the efficient market hypothesis is very close to the technical premise that markets discount everything.

related_concepts: Random Walk Theory Rejection by Technicians
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Markets Discount Everything
concepts.body: A foundational premise of technical analysis is that important market information is discounted in the market price long before it becomes publicly known. This makes monitoring price action more valuable than acting on fundamental information, especially over the short term.

quotes.text: the basis of technical forecasting, already touched upon, is that important market information is discounted in the market price long before it becomes known.

related_concepts: Efficient Market Hypothesis vs. Technical Premise
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Futility of Short-Term Fundamental Trading
concepts.body: The author argues, drawing on the academics' own EMH logic, that trying to profit from fundamental information is futile, at least over the short term, because that information is already embedded in price. This represents a stated limitation of fundamental analysis relative to technical analysis in short-term trading.

quotes.text: the academics have very eloquently stated the need for closely monitoring price action and the futility of trying to profit from fundamental information, at least over the short term.

related_concepts: Efficient Market Hypothesis vs. Technical Premise
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Illusion of Randomness in Markets
concepts.body: The author argues that markets only appear random to those who have not studied the rules of market behavior. Just as an electrocardiogram appears as random noise to a layperson but is meaningful to a trained physician, price charts become interpretable with skill development. The illusion of randomness diminishes as chart-reading skill improves.

quotes.text: any process appears random and unpredictable to those who do not understand the rules under which that process operates.
The illusion of randomness gradually disappears as the skill in chart reading improves.

related_concepts: Random Walk Theory Rejection by Technicians
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Behavioral Finance and Technical Analysis
concepts.body: Behavioral Finance, increasingly explored by leading universities, maintains that human psychology and securities pricing are intertwined. The author notes this is the primary basis of technical analysis, suggesting academic finance is converging toward the technical analyst's worldview.

quotes.text: Behavioral Finance which maintains that human psychology and securities pricing are intertwined. That, of course, is the primary basis of technical analysis.

related_concepts: Random Walk Theory Rejection by Technicians
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Universal Applicability of Technical Principles
concepts.body: Technical principles discussed in the book can be applied universally to all markets, including futures, stocks, mutual funds, and sector indices. Originally developed primarily for futures markets, these tools have been widely adopted in stock market analysis over the past decade.

quotes.text: The technical principles that are discussed in this book can be applied universally to all markets—even mutual funds.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Sector Investing with Technical Tools
concepts.body: Sector investing, primarily through index options and mutual funds, has gained wide popularity. The author indicates that technical timing tools can be used to determine which market sectors are performing well and which are not, extending the application of technical analysis beyond individual securities.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Dow Theory as Foundation of Technical Analysis
concepts.body: Dow Theory, originating from Charles Dow's editorials in the Wall Street Journal around the turn of the century, forms the cornerstone of technical analysis. Most technicians today recognize and assimilate Dow's basic ideas, and the theory remains foundational even amid modern computer technology and newer technical indicators.

quotes.text: Dow Theory still forms the cornerstone of the study of technical analysis, even in the face of today's sophisticated computer technology, and the proliferation of newer and supposedly better technical indicators.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Origins of Dow Jones Stock Market Averages
concepts.body: Charles Dow published the first stock market average on July 3, 1884, composed of closing prices of eleven stocks — nine railroad companies and two manufacturing firms — as an indicator of the country's economic health. By 1897, Dow created two separate indices (a 12-stock industrial index and a 20-stock rail index) to better represent economic health, evolving into the modern Dow Jones averages.

quotes.text: On July 3, 1884, Dow published the first stock market average composed of the closing prices of eleven stocks: nine railroad companies and two manufacturing firms.

related_concepts: Dow Theory as Foundation of Technical Analysis
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Markets Discount All Known Information
concepts.body: Dow's first tenet holds that the stock market averages reflect all known information affecting supply and demand, including economic data, news, and even acts of God. While markets cannot anticipate natural disasters, they rapidly assimilate such events into price action. This is a foundational premise of technical analysis.

quotes.text: The sum and tendency of the transactions of the Stock Exchange represent the sum of all Wall Street's knowledge of the past, immediate and remote, applied to the discounting of the future.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Dow's Three-Tier Trend Classification
concepts.body: Dow identified three distinct trend timeframes: the primary trend (the tide), lasting more than a year and possibly several years; the secondary or intermediate trend (the waves), lasting three weeks to three months; and the minor trend (ripples), lasting less than three weeks. Each shorter trend is a component of the larger one.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Definition of Uptrend and Downtrend
concepts.body: Dow defined an uptrend as a series of successively higher rally highs and higher rally lows — a pattern of rising peaks and troughs. A downtrend is the opposite: successively lower peaks and troughs. This definition remains the cornerstone of trend analysis in modern technical work.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Secondary Trend Retracement Range
concepts.body: Secondary or intermediate trends represent corrections within the primary trend. According to Dow, these corrections generally retrace between one-third and two-thirds of the previous trend movement, with the most common retracement being approximately 50% of the prior move.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Three Phases of a Major Bull Market
concepts.body: Dow identified three phases in a primary bull market: (1) the accumulation phase, where informed investors buy while bad news is still prevalent; (2) the public participation phase, where prices advance rapidly and business news improves; and (3) the distribution phase, where bullish sentiment is widespread, speculative volume peaks, and informed investors begin selling to the public.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Elliott Wave Three-Phase Parallel
concepts.body: The author notes that Elliott Wave Theory's structure of three major upward movements in a bull market closely mirrors Dow's three phases. R.N. Elliott elaborated on Rhea's Dow Theory work to identify five-wave sequences, with the three upward waves corresponding to Dow's accumulation, public participation, and distribution phases.
related_concepts: Three Phases of a Major Bull Market
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Dow Averages Must Confirm Each Other
concepts.body: Dow's fourth tenet states that no important bull or bear market signal is valid unless both the Industrial and Rail (Transport) Averages give the same signal. Both averages must exceed a previous secondary peak to confirm a bull market. When the two averages diverge, the prior trend is assumed to remain in effect. Signals need not occur simultaneously, but closer timing provides stronger confirmation.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Confirmation and Divergence
concepts.body: When two market averages (Industrials and Transports) move in the same direction and confirm each other's signals, it strengthens the validity of the trend signal. When they diverge — one making a new high while the other does not — the prior trend is assumed to continue. Divergence is a warning sign rather than a reversal signal on its own.
related_concepts: Dow Averages Must Confirm Each Other
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Volume Confirms the Trend
concepts.body: Dow's fifth tenet holds that volume should expand in the direction of the major trend. In an uptrend, volume should increase as prices rise and diminish on pullbacks. In a downtrend, volume should increase as prices fall and diminish on rallies. Dow treated volume as a secondary confirming indicator, basing actual buy and sell signals solely on closing prices.
related_concepts: Dow Averages Must Confirm Each Other
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Trend Continuation Assumption
concepts.body: Dow's sixth tenet states that a trend is assumed to remain in effect until it gives definite signals of reversal. This principle, analogous to Newton's law of inertia, means the odds favor continuation of the existing trend. Reversal signals can be identified through support/resistance levels, price patterns, trendlines, and moving averages.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Failure Swing Reversal Signal
concepts.body: A failure swing occurs when a rally peak (C) fails to exceed the previous peak (A), and price then declines below the prior trough (B). The presence of two lower peaks and two lower troughs constitutes a clear sell signal at the point where the low at B is broken. This is considered a definitive reversal signal under Dow Theory.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Nonfailure Swing Reversal Signal
concepts.body: A nonfailure swing occurs when a rally top (C) exceeds the previous peak (A) before price declines below the prior trough (B). Some Dow theorists consider the violation of support at B (S1) a sell signal; others require a subsequent lower high (E) followed by a new low under D (S2) before confirming a reversal. This ambiguity is a known source of disagreement among Dow theorists.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Distinguishing Correction from New Trend
concepts.body: The most difficult challenge for a Dow theorist or any trend-follower is distinguishing between a normal secondary correction within an existing trend and the first leg of a new trend in the opposite direction. Dow theorists frequently disagree on when an actual reversal signal has been given, as illustrated by the difference between failure and nonfailure swing interpretations.
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Arithmetic vs. Logarithmic Price Scales
concepts.body: Arithmetic scales show equal distances for equal price moves in absolute terms, so a move from 50 to 55 looks the same as a move from 1 to 6. Logarithmic (ratio) scales show equal distances for equal percentage moves, so a doubling from 10 to 20 covers the same vertical distance as a doubling from 20 to 40. Many stock chart services use log charts while futures chart services typically use arithmetic scales.

quotes.text: Prices plotted on ratio or log scales show equal distances for similar percentage moves.
a move from 10 to 20 (a 100% increase) would be the same distance on a log chart as a move from 20 to 40 or 40 to 80.

related_concepts: Trendline Accuracy on Log vs. Arithmetic Scale
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Trendline Accuracy on Log vs. Arithmetic Scale
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] The author notes that for longer-term charts, the logarithmic scale can produce more accurate trendlines. The example given is Intel's three-year up trendline, which worked better on the log chart than on the arithmetic chart.

quotes.text: Notice that the three year up trendline worked better on the log chart.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Daily Bar Chart Construction
concepts.body: A daily bar chart plots price on the vertical (y) axis and time on the horizontal (x) axis. Each day is represented by a vertical bar spanning the day's high to low (the range). The closing price is marked by a small horizontal tic to the right of the bar, and the opening price is marked by a tic to the left. Volume is plotted as vertical bars along the bottom of the chart.

quotes.text: All the user has to do is plot a vertical bar in the appropriate day from the day's high to the day's low (called the range). Place a horizontal tic to the right of the vertical bar identifying the daily closing price.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Opening vs. Closing Price Tic Placement
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] The closing price tic is placed to the right of the vertical bar, while the opening price tic is placed to the left. This convention distinguishes the two data points visually on the bar chart.

quotes.text: The reason for placing the tic to the right of the bar is to distinguish it from the opening price, which chartists record to the left of the bar.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Volume on Bar Charts
concepts.body: Volume represents the total trading activity in a market for a given day — the total number of futures contracts traded or stock shares that changed hands. It is plotted as a vertical bar at the bottom of the chart beneath the corresponding day's price bar. A taller volume bar indicates heavier trading activity; a shorter bar indicates lighter activity.

quotes.text: Volume represents the total amount of trading activity in that market for that day. It is the total number of futures contracts traded during the day or the number of common stock shares that change hands on a given day in the stock market.
A higher volume bar means the volume was heavier for that day. A smaller bar represents lighter volume.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Open Interest in Futures
concepts.body: Open interest is the total number of outstanding futures contracts held by market participants at the end of the day. Because every long position must have a corresponding short, open interest is counted only on one side (either longs or shorts, not both combined). It is plotted on the chart as a solid line, typically above the volume bars but below the price bars.

quotes.text: Open interest is the total number of outstanding futures contracts that are held by market participants at the end of the day. Open interest is the number of outstanding contracts held by the longs or the shorts, not the total of both.
Open interest is marked on the chart with a solid line along the bottom, usually just above the volume but below the price.

related_concepts: Total vs. Individual Volume and Open Interest in Futures
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Total vs. Individual Volume and Open Interest in Futures
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] Futures technicians use total volume and open interest figures across all delivery months rather than figures for individual contract months. This is because individual contract figures are distorted by the contract lifecycle: volume and open interest are low early in a contract's life, build toward maturity, and then decline again as expiration approaches and traders liquidate positions.

quotes.text: Futures chart services, along with most futures technicians, use only the total volume and open interest figures.

In the early stages of a futures contract's life, volume and open interest are usually quite small. The figures build up as the contract reaches maturity. In the last couple of months before expiration, however, the numbers begin to drop again.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Weekend and Holiday Gaps on Bar Charts
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] Most chart services use five-day trading weeks and do not show weekends on the chart. When an exchange is closed during the trading week (e.g., a holiday), that day's space is left blank on the chart.

quotes.text: Most chart services use five day weeks. Weekends are not shown on the chart. Whenever an exchange is closed during the trading week, that day's space is left blank.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Chart Patterns as Human Psychology
concepts.body: Chart patterns are not merely technical pictures; they reflect the actual behavior and psychology of market participants. Chart analysis is fundamentally a study of human psychology and how traders react to changing market conditions. The author warns that reliance on shorthand terminology can obscure the underlying human forces that create chart patterns.

quotes.text: Chart analysis is actually a study of human psychology and the reactions of traders to changing market conditions.
There are sound psychological reasons why support and resistance levels can be identified on price charts and why they can be used to help predict market movements.

related_concepts: Psychological Basis of Support and Resistance
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Support and Resistance Role Reversal
concepts.body: Once a support level is decisively penetrated to the downside, it reverses roles and becomes a resistance level on subsequent bounces. Conversely, once a resistance level is decisively broken to the upside, it becomes a new support level on subsequent pullbacks. This role reversal is driven by the shift in trader psychology — previous sellers near a resistance peak become buyers once that level is broken.

quotes.text: A support level, penetrated by a significant margin, becomes a resistance level and vice versa.
All of the previous selling near the top of wave 1 (creating the resistance level) has now become buying under the market.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Significance of Penetration for Role Reversal
concepts.body: Role reversal of support and resistance only occurs after a significant penetration of the level. The greater the distance prices travel away from a support or resistance level, the more significant that level becomes. However, determining what constitutes a 'significant' penetration involves a degree of subjectivity, and the author acknowledges this as a benchmark challenge.

quotes.text: support and resistance levels reverse roles only after a significant penetration. But what constitutes significant? There is quite a bit of subjectivity involved here in determining whether a penetration is significant or not.
the distance prices traveled away from support or resistance increased the significance of that support or resistance.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Previous Peaks as Future Support in Uptrend
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] In an uptrend, once a prior resistance peak is decisively broken, that level transforms into a support zone. On subsequent corrections, prices tend to find support at or near that prior resistance peak. This is illustrated by the Nasdaq 100 example where a 1997 resistance peak became support during a later intermediate decline.

quotes.text: In an uptrend, resistance levels that have been broken by a significant margin become support levels. Notice that once resistance at point 1 is exceeded, it provides support at point 4. Previous peaks function as support on subsequent corrections.

related_concepts: Significance of Penetration for Role Reversal
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Previous Support as Future Resistance in Downtrend
concepts.body: [Paraphrase] In a downtrend, once a prior support level is violated to the downside, it reverses roles and acts as a resistance ceiling on subsequent bounces. Traders who previously bought at that support level and are now holding losing positions will tend to sell on any rally back to that level, reinforcing its new role as resistance.

quotes.text: In a downtrend, violated Support levels become resistance levels on subsequent bounces. Notice how previous support at point 1 became resistance at point 4.

related_concepts: Significance of Penetration for Role Reversal
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Subjectivity in Defining Significant Penetration
concepts.body: The rule that support and resistance reverse roles only after a significant penetration has an inherent limitation: determining what is 'significant' is subjective. The author acknowledges this openly, noting that some benchmark is needed but that there is no universally precise definition. This subjectivity is a known weakness in applying the role-reversal principle.

quotes.text: There is quite a bit of subjectivity involved here in determining whether a penetration is significant or not.

related_concepts: Significance of Penetration for Role Reversal
concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Psychological Basis of Support and Resistance
concepts.body: Support and resistance levels exist on charts because of the collective psychology of market participants. Traders who sold near a resistance level create selling pressure that defines resistance; once that level is broken, those same traders become buyers on dips, converting resistance into support. This psychological mechanism gives support and resistance levels their predictive power.

quotes.text: There are sound psychological reasons why support and resistance levels can be identified on price charts and why they can be used to help predict market movements.

concepts.source_book: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets

concepts.title: Round Numbers as Psychological Support and Resistance
concepts.body: Markets tend to stall at round numbers such as 10, 20, 25, 50, 75, 100, and multiples of 1000. Traders naturally think in terms of round numbers as price objectives, causing these levels to act as psychological support or resistance. Historical examples include gold stalling at $300, $400, and $500, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average stalling at multiples of 1000.
quotes: There is a tendency for round numbers to stop advances or declines.
These round numbers, therefore, will often act as 'psychological' support or resistance levels.

concepts.title: Taking Profits Near Round Numbers
concepts.body: A trader can use the tendency of round numbers to act as support or resistance to begin taking profits as an important round number is approached. This is a practical application of the psychological significance of round numbers in price action.
quotes: A trader can use this information to begin taking profits as an important round number is approached.

concepts.title: Placing Limit Buy Orders Just Above Round Numbers
concepts.body: When trying to buy into a short-term dip in an uptrend, limit buy orders should be placed just above an important round number rather than at the round number itself. Because many other traders are also trying to buy at the round number, the market may never reach that exact level, so placing orders slightly above increases the chance of execution.
quotes: if the trader is trying to buy into a short term market dip in an uptrend, it would make sense to place limit orders just above an important round number.

concepts.title: Placing Sell Orders Just Below Round Numbers
concepts.body: Traders looking to sell on a bounce should place resting sell orders just below round numbers, not at the round number itself. This accounts for the tendency of markets to stall just before reaching a round number, ensuring the sell order is more likely to be filled.
quotes: Traders looking to sell on a bounce should place resting sell orders just below round numbers.

concepts.title: Protective Stop Placement Relative to Round Numbers
concepts.body: As a general rule, protective stops should never be placed at obvious round numbers. Protective stops on long positions should be placed below round numbers, and stops on short positions should be placed above round numbers. This avoids the common mistake of placing stops at levels where many other traders also have orders, increasing the risk of being stopped out prematurely.
quotes: As a general rule, avoid placing protective stops at obvious round numbers.
protective stops on long positions should be placed below round numbers and on short positions, above such numbers.

concepts.title: Definition of an Up Trendline
concepts.body: An up trendline is a straight line drawn upward to the right along successive reaction lows. It requires at least two reaction lows with the second low higher than the first to be drawn. The trendline is projected up and to the right once two ascending lows have been identified.
quotes: An up trendline is a straight line drawn upward to the right along successive reaction lows.

concepts.title: Definition of a Down Trendline
concepts.body: A down trendline is drawn downward to the right along successive rally peaks. It requires at least two successively lower rally highs to be drawn. The same rules for confirmation apply as with an up trendline.
quotes: A down trendline is drawn downward to the right along successive rally peaks.

concepts.title: Tentative vs. Valid Trendline: The Third Point Rule
concepts.body: A trendline drawn through only two points is considered tentative. To confirm the validity of a trendline, it must be touched a third time with prices bouncing off of it. Two points are needed to draw the trendline, and a third point is required to make it a valid trendline.
quotes: two points are needed to draw the trendline, and a third point to make it a valid trendline.

concepts.title: Trendline as a Buying Area in an Uptrend
concepts.body: In an uptrend, corrective dips will often touch or come very close to the up trendline. Because the intent of the trader is to buy dips in an uptrend, the trendline provides a support boundary under the market that can be used as a buying area. This approach is valid as long as the trendline has not been violated.
quotes: that trendline provides a support boundary under the market that can be used as a buying area.

concepts.title: Trendline as a Selling Area in a Downtrend
concepts.body: A down trendline can be used as a resistance area for selling purposes. Rallies back to the down trendline provide opportunities to initiate or add to short positions as long as the trendline remains intact.
quotes: A down trendline can be used as a resistance area for selling purposes.

concepts.title: Trendline Violation as a Trend Change Signal
concepts.body: When a trendline is violated (broken), it signals a change in trend and calls for liquidation of all positions in the direction of the previous trend. The breaking of the trendline is one of the best early warnings of a change in trend. This signal applies to both up and down trendlines.
quotes: the violation of the trendline signals a trend change, calling for liquidation of all positions in the direction of the previous trend.
Very often, the breaking of the trendline is one of the best early warnings of a change in trend.

concepts.title: Significance of a Trendline: Length and Number of Tests
concepts.body: The significance of a trendline is determined by two factors: how long it has been intact and how many times it has been tested. A trendline tested eight times is more significant than one tested three times, and a trendline in effect for nine months is more important than one in effect for nine weeks. The more significant the trendline, the more important its penetration.
quotes: The answer to that question is twofold—the longer it has been intact and the number of times it has been tested.
The more significant the trendline, the more confidence it inspires and the more important is its penetration.

concepts.title: Trendlines Should Include the Full Price Range
concepts.body: Trendlines on bar charts should be drawn over or under the entire day's price range, not just closing prices. While the closing price is the most important price of the day, it represents only a small sample of that day's activity. Including the full daily range is the standard and more common procedure.
quotes: Trendlines on bar charts should be drawn over or under the entire day's price range.

concepts.title: Handling Intraday Trendline Penetrations
concepts.body: When prices violate a trendline on an intraday basis but then close back in the direction of the original trend, the trendline may still be considered valid. A compromise approach is to keep the original trendline but draw a new dotted line until it can be better determined which is the truer line. There is no hard and fast rule for this situation.
quotes: Sometimes it is best to ignore the minor breach, especially if subsequent market action proves that the original line is still valid.

concepts.title: Closing Penetration Rule for Valid Trendline Break
concepts.body: As a general rule, a close beyond the trendline is more significant than just an intraday penetration. A closing penetration provides a stronger signal of a genuine trendline break. Intraday violations alone are generally insufficient to confirm a trendline break.
quotes: As a general rule, a close beyond the trendline is more significant than just an intraday penetration.

concepts.title: 3% Price Filter for Trendline Penetration
concepts.body: A common price filter for confirming a valid trendline break is the 3% penetration criterion, used mainly for longer-term trendlines. It requires that the trendline be broken on a closing basis by at least 3% of the price level where the line was broken. This filter is not appropriate for shorter-term trading or some financial futures such as interest rate markets, where a 1% criterion may be more suitable.
quotes: One example of a price filter is the 3% penetration criteria.
requires that the trendline be broken, on a closing basis, by at least 3%.

concepts.title: Tradeoff in Filter Size for Trendline Signals
concepts.body: There is a tradeoff in the use of any type of filter for trendline penetrations. If the filter is too small, it will not effectively reduce whipsaws. If the filter is too large, much of the initial move will be missed before a valid signal is given. The trader must determine the appropriate filter based on the degree of trend being followed and the characteristics of the individual market.
quotes: If the filter is too small, it won't be very useful in reducing the impact of whipsaws. If it's too big, then much of the initial move will be missed before a valid signal is given.

concepts.title: 3% Penetration Criteria for Support and Resistance
concepts.body: Many chartists use a 3% penetration as a criterion for confirming a break of major support and resistance levels. Shorter-term support and resistance areas may require a smaller number, such as 1%. The key principle is that support and resistance areas only reverse roles when the market moves far enough away to convince participants they have made a mistake.
quotes: It's important to remember, however, that support and resistance areas only reverse roles when the market moves far enough away to convince the market participants that they have made a mistake.
The farther away the market moves, the more convinced they become.

concepts.title: Trend Maintains Its Slope Over Time
concepts.body: Once a trend assumes a certain slope or rate of speed as identified by the trendline, it will usually maintain the same slope. This is a corollary to the principle that a trend in motion tends to remain in motion. The trendline helps determine the extremities of corrective phases and signals when the trend is changing.
quotes: once a trend assumes a certain slope or rate of speed, as identified by the trendline, it will usually maintain the same slope.

concepts.title: Time Filter for Trendline Breaks (Two-Day Rule)
concepts.body: A valid trendline break requires prices to close beyond the trendline for two successive days. A single-day violation does not count as a confirmed break. This rule applies to both up and down trendlines as well as important support and resistance levels.
quotes: to have a valid breaking of a trendline, prices must close beyond the trendline for two successive days

concepts.title: Price Filter for Trendline Breaks (1-3% Rule)
concepts.body: An alternative to the two-day time filter is a price filter requiring prices to penetrate a trendline by a predetermined price increment or percentage amount, commonly 1-3%. This rule applies to both trendlines and important support and resistance levels.
quotes: The 1-3% rule and the two day rule are also applied to the breaking of important support and resistance levels, not just to major trendlines.

concepts.title: Weekly Close Filter for Breakouts
concepts.body: Another filter requires a Friday close beyond a major breakout point to ensure a valid weekly signal. This adds an additional confirmation layer beyond daily price action.
quotes: Another filter would require a Friday close beyond a major breakout point to ensure a weekly signal.

concepts.title: Trendline Role Reversal
concepts.body: Once a trendline is decisively broken, it reverses its role. An up trendline (support line) becomes a resistance line after being broken to the downside. A down trendline (resistance line) becomes a support line after being broken to the upside. Trendlines should be projected as far to the right as possible even after being broken, as old trendlines frequently act as support or resistance again in the future.
quotes: an up trendline (a support line) will usually become a resistance line once it's decisively broken
It's surprising how often old trendlines act as support and resistance lines again in the future, but in the opposite role.

concepts.title: Trendline Measuring Implication (Price Objective)
concepts.body: Once a trendline is broken, prices will typically move a distance beyond the trendline equal to the vertical distance prices achieved on the other side of the line prior to the trend reversal. For example, if prices moved $50 above an up trendline during the uptrend, they would be expected to drop $50 below the trendline after it is broken.
quotes: once a trendline is broken, prices will usually move a distance beyond the trendline equal to the vertical distance that prices achieved on the other side of the line, prior to the trend reversal.

concepts.title: Fan Principle
concepts.body: After a trendline violation, prices may decline briefly before rallying back to the broken trendline (now resistance), forming a second trendline. After another failed rally, a third trendline is drawn. The breaking of the third trendline is the valid trend reversal signal. The lines gradually flatten out, resembling a fan. Previously broken support lines become resistance and resistance lines become support throughout this process.
quotes: The breaking of that third trendline is usually an indication that prices are headed lower.
the breaking of the third line is the valid trend reversal signal.

concepts.title: Fan Principle at a Bottom
concepts.body: At a market bottom, three successive down trendlines are drawn as prices make lower rally attempts. The breaking of the third down trendline constitutes a new uptrend signal. Previously broken resistance lines become support levels during this process.
quotes: the breaking of the third down trendline (line 3) constitutes a new uptrend signal.

concepts.title: The Number Three in Technical Analysis
concepts.body: The number three appears repeatedly throughout technical analysis: the fan principle uses three lines; major bull and bear markets have three phases; there are three kinds of gaps; reversal patterns like triple tops and head and shoulders have three peaks; there are three trend classifications (major, secondary, minor) and three trend directions; there are three types of triangles; and there are three principal sources of information (price, volume, open interest).
quotes: the number three plays a very prominent role throughout the entire field of technical analysis.

concepts.title: Optimal Trendline Slope (45-Degree Rule)
concepts.body: Most important up trendlines tend to approximate an average slope of 45 degrees, reflecting a balance between price and time. A trendline that is too steep indicates an unsustainable rate of ascent, while a trendline that is too flat suggests the uptrend is weak and suspect. The 45-degree line technique was favored by W.D. Gann.
quotes: most important up trendlines tend to approximate an average slope of 45 degrees
Such a line reflects a situation where prices are advancing or declining at such a rate that price and time are in perfect balance.

concepts.title: Steep Trendline Break Interpretation
concepts.body: When a steep trendline is broken, it may not signal a full trend reversal but rather an adjustment to a more sustainable slope closer to 45 degrees. A new, less steep trendline should be drawn to track the continuing trend.
quotes: The breaking of that steep trendline may be just a reaction back to a more sustainable slope closer to the 45 degree line

concepts.title: Adjusting Trendlines for Accelerating Trends
concepts.body: Trendlines must be adjusted when a trend accelerates or decelerates. If the original trendline is too flat, it must be redrawn at a steeper angle. For accelerating trends requiring increasingly steep trendlines, a moving average (curvilinear trendline) is preferable to drawing multiple steeper trendlines.
quotes: where steeper trendlines become necessary, it is best to resort to another tool—the moving average—which is the same as a curvilinear trendline.

concepts.title: Multiple Trendlines for Different Trend Degrees
concepts.body: Different trendlines are needed to measure different degrees of trend simultaneously. A major up trendline connects the low points of the major uptrend, while shorter, more sensitive lines measure secondary swings and short-term movements. Technicians use many different trendlines on the same chart.
quotes: Just as there are several different degrees of trend in effect at any one time, so is there a need for different trendlines to measure those various trends.

concepts.title: Channel Line (Return Line)
concepts.body: A channel line is drawn parallel to the basic trendline from the first prominent peak (in an uptrend) or trough (in a downtrend), forming a price channel. In an uptrend, the basic trendline is drawn along the lows and the channel line is projected over the first peak, parallel to the basic trendline. If the next rally reaches and backs off from the channel line and prices then return to the original trendline, a channel is confirmed.
quotes: Sometimes prices trend between two parallel lines—the basic trendline and the channel line.

If the next rally reaches and backs off from the channel line (at point 4), then a channel may exist. If prices then drop back to the original trendline (at point 5), then a channel probably does exist.

concepts.title: Channel Line Trading Application
concepts.body: When a price channel is identified, the channel line serves as a profit target for trades initiated at the basic trendline. Prices will often remain within such a trend channel, making the channel boundaries actionable levels for entries and exits.
quotes: when this is the case and when the analyst recognizes that a channel exists, this knowledge can be used to profitable advantage.

concepts.title: Channel Line as Profit-Taking Zone
concepts.body: The upper channel line in an uptrend can be used for short-term profit taking on long positions. When price reaches the upper parallel channel line, traders holding long positions initiated near the basic trendline can use this level to exit or reduce exposure. This technique leverages the tendency of prices to oscillate between the two parallel boundaries of a channel.
quotes: The channel line can be used for short term profit taking.

concepts.title: Basic Trendline as Long Entry Zone
concepts.body: The basic up trendline (lower boundary of an ascending channel) can be used for the initiation of new long positions. When price pulls back to the basic trendline within an established channel, it presents a buying opportunity aligned with the prevailing trend. This approach uses the channel structure to time entries at favorable risk/reward levels.
quotes: The basic up trendline can be used for the initiation of new long positions.

concepts.title: Countertrend Short at Channel Line — High Risk
concepts.body: More aggressive traders might use the upper channel line to initiate a countertrend short position, but this is explicitly flagged as dangerous and usually costly. Trading against the prevailing trend is a high-risk tactic even when price is at a logical resistance level. This warning applies broadly to any attempt to fade a strong trend at channel extremes.
quotes: trading in the opposite direction of the prevailing trend can be a dangerous and usually costly tactic.

concepts.title: Channel Reliability Increases with Time and Tests
concepts.body: The longer a channel remains intact and the more often it is successfully tested, the more important and reliable it becomes. This mirrors the principle applied to basic trendlines — repeated validation at both boundaries increases the statistical significance of the channel. Traders should give more weight to well-established, long-duration channels.
quotes: the longer the channel remains intact and the more often it is successfully tested, the more important and reliable it becomes.

concepts.title: Breaking the Major Trendline vs. Breaking the Channel Line
concepts.body: Breaking the major (basic) trendline signals an important change in trend, while breaking the upper channel line in an uptrend has the opposite meaning — it signals an acceleration of the existing trend. These two events must not be confused, as they carry diametrically opposite implications for trade management. Some traders view clearing the upper channel line as a reason to add to long positions.
quotes: The breaking of the major trendline indicates an important change in trend.
the breaking of a rising channel line has exactly the opposite meaning, and signals an acceleration of the existing trend.
Some traders view the clearing of the upper line in an uptrend as a reason to add to long positions.

concepts.title: Failure to Reach Channel Line as Early Warning Signal
concepts.body: When price fails to reach the upper channel line during an uptrend, it is often an early warning that the trend is weakening and that the basic trendline (lower boundary) may soon be broken. This failure indicates diminishing momentum and shifts the odds toward a trend reversal. Traders should monitor whether each successive rally reaches the upper channel boundary.
quotes: the failure of prices to reach the top of the channel (at point 5) may be an early warning that the trend is turning, and increases the odds that the other line (the basic up trendline) will be broken.

concepts.title: General Rule: Failure to Reach One Side Signals Likely Break of Other Side
concepts.body: As a general rule of thumb, the failure of any move within an established price channel to reach one side of the channel indicates that the trend is shifting and increases the likelihood that the other side of the channel will be broken. This principle applies in both uptrends and downtrends and serves as an early diagnostic tool for trend deterioration. It is actionable as a warning to tighten stops or reduce position size.
quotes: the failure of any move within an established price channel to reach one side of the channel usually indicates that the trend is shifting, and increases the likelihood that the other side of the channel will be broken.

concepts.title: Adjusting the Trendline Upward When Price Breaks Above Channel
concepts.body: If prices move above a projected rising channel line by a significant amount, it signals a strengthening trend. In this case, chartists draw a steeper basic up trendline from the last reaction low, parallel to the new (higher) channel line. The new steeper support line often functions better than the original flatter trendline as the trend accelerates.
quotes: If prices move above a projected rising channel line by a significant amount, it usually indicates a strengthening trend.
Often, the new steeper support line functions better than the old flatter line.

concepts.title: Adjusting the Trendline Downward When Price Fails to Reach Upper Channel
concepts.body: When an uptrend fails to reach the upper end of its channel, this justifies drawing a new, flatter support line under the last reaction low, parallel to a new resistance line drawn over the past two peaks. This adjustment reflects the reduced momentum of the trend and recalibrates the channel to current price behavior. It provides a more accurate framework for managing the trade going forward.
quotes: the failure of an uptrend to reach the upper end of a channel justifies the drawing of a new support line under the last reaction low parallel to the new resistance line over the past two peaks.

concepts.title: Long-Term Channel Validity — Historical Example
concepts.body: A monthly line chart example demonstrates that prices can fluctuate between upper and lower parallel channel lines over a period of 25 years, with major tops in 1987, 1989, and 1993 occurring right at the upper channel line and the 1994 bottom bouncing off the lower trendline. This illustrates that channel lines can remain valid across very long time horizons. Long-term channels on monthly charts carry especially high significance for major turning points.
quotes: Notice how prices fluctuated between the upper and lower parallel channels over a period of 25 years.
The 1987, 1989, and 1993 tops occurred right at the upper channel line. The 1994 bottom bounced off the lower trendline.

concepts.title: Channel Line Measuring Implication
concepts.body: Once a breakout occurs from an existing price channel, prices usually travel a distance equal to the width of the channel. The user measures the width of the channel and projects that amount from the point at which either trendline is broken. This provides a price target after a channel breakout.
quotes: Once a breakout occurs from an existing price channel, prices usually travel a distance equal to the width of the channel.

concepts.title: Basic Trendline vs. Channel Line Importance
concepts.body: Of the two lines in a channel, the basic trendline is by far the more important and more reliable. The channel line is a secondary use of the trendline technique. However, the channel line works often enough to justify its inclusion in the chartist's toolkit.
quotes: the basic trendline is by far the more important and the more reliable. The channel line is a secondary use of the trendline technique.

concepts.title: Percentage Retracements — General Principle
concepts.body: After a market move, prices retrace a portion of the previous trend before resuming in the original direction. These countertrend moves tend to fall into certain predictable percentage parameters. Percentage retracements apply to any degree of trend — major, secondary, and near term.
quotes: These countertrend moves tend to fall into certain predictable percentage parameters.

concepts.title: 50% Retracement
concepts.body: The best-known retracement is the 50% retracement. If a market trends from 100 to 200, the subsequent reaction very often retraces about half of the prior move to the 150 level before upward momentum is regained. This is a very well-known market tendency that happens quite frequently.
quotes: the subsequent reaction retraces about half of the prior move, to about the 150 level, before upward momentum is regained.

concepts.title: One-Third and Two-Thirds Retracement Parameters
concepts.body: Besides the 50% retracement, the minimum retracement is about 33% and the maximum about 66%. In a correction of a strong trend, the market usually retraces at least a third of the previous move. A trader looking for a buying area can compute a 33–50% zone on the chart and use that price zone as a general frame of reference for buying opportunities.
quotes: a minimum retracement is about 33% and a maximum about 66%
in a correction of a strong trend, the market usually retraces at least a third of the previous move.

concepts.title: Two-Thirds Retracement as Critical Reversal Level
concepts.body: The maximum retracement parameter of 66% is an especially critical area. If the prior trend is to be maintained, the correction must stop at the two-thirds point. This becomes a relatively low-risk buying area in an uptrend or selling area in a downtrend. If prices move beyond the two-thirds point, the odds favor a trend reversal rather than just a retracement, and the move usually retraces the entire 100% of the prior trend.
quotes: If the prior trend is to be maintained, the correction must stop at the two-thirds point.
If prices move beyond the two-thirds point, the odds then favor a trend reversal rather than just a retracement.

concepts.title: Combined Retracement Zones (Dow + Fibonacci)
concepts.body: Combining Dow Theory retracements (33%, 50%, 66%) with Elliott Wave/Fibonacci retracements (38%, 62%) yields a minimum retracement zone of 33–38% and a maximum zone of 62–66%. Some technicians round these off further to arrive at a 40–60% retracement zone.
quotes: I prefer to combine both approaches for a minimum retracement zone of 33-38% and a maximum zone of 62-66%.

concepts.title: Fibonacci Retracement Levels (38% and 62%)
concepts.body: Followers of Elliott Wave Theory and Fibonacci ratios use percentage retracements of 38% and 62%. These levels are popular with chartists and most corrections will find support in the 38% to 50% retracement zones. The 38% and 62% lines are Fibonacci retracements.
quotes: Most corrections will find support in the 38% to 50% retracement zones. The 38% and 62% lines are Fibonacci retracements and are popular with chartists.

concepts.title: Gann's Retracement Eighths
concepts.body: W.D. Gann broke down the trend structure into eighths — 1/8, 2/8, 3/8, 4/8, 5/8, 6/8, 7/8, 8/8. Even Gann attached special importance to the 3/8 (38%), 4/8 (50%), and 5/8 (62%) retracement numbers and also felt it was important to divide the trend into thirds — 1/3 (33%) and 2/3 (66%).
quotes: even Gann attached special importance to the 3/8 (38%), 4/8 (50%), and 5/8 (62%) retracement numbers

concepts.title: Speed Resistance Lines — Construction
concepts.body: Speed resistance lines (speedlines), developed by Edson Gould, measure the rate of ascent or descent of a trend. To construct a bullish speedline, find the highest point in the current uptrend, draw a vertical line from that high to where the trend began, divide that vertical line into thirds, and draw trendlines from the beginning of the trend through the one-third and two-thirds points. In a downtrend, reverse the process using the lowest point.
quotes: speed resistance lines (or speedlines) measure the rate of ascent or descent of a trend (in other words, its speed).

concepts.title: Speed Resistance Lines — Support and Resistance Rules
concepts.body: In an uptrend correction, the downside correction will usually stop at the higher speedline (2/3 speedline). If not, prices will drop to the lower speedline (1/3 speedline). If the lower line is also broken, prices will probably continue all the way to the beginning of the prior trend. In a downtrend, breaking the lower line indicates a probable rally to the higher line, and if that is broken, a rally to the top of the prior trend is indicated.
quotes: If an uptrend is in the process of correcting itself, the downside correction will usually stop at the higher speedline (the 2/3 speedline).

concepts.title: Speed Resistance Lines — Role Reversal
concepts.body: As with all trendlines, speedlines reverse roles once they are broken. During the correction of an uptrend, if the upper line (2/3 line) is broken and prices fall to the 1/3 line and rally from there, that upper line becomes a resistance barrier. Only when that upper line is broken would a signal be given that the old highs will probably be challenged.
quotes: As with all trendlines, speedlines reverse roles once they are broken.

concepts.title: Speed Resistance Lines — Redrawing on New Highs/Lows
concepts.body: Each time a new high is set in an uptrend or a new low in a downtrend, a new set of speedlines must be drawn because there is now a new high or low point. Because speedlines are drawn from the beginning of the trend through the one-third and two-thirds points, those trendlines may sometimes move through some of the price action.
quotes: Each time a new high is set in an uptrend or a new low in a downtrend, a new set of lines must be drawn

concepts.title: Fibonacci Fan Lines
concepts.body: Fibonacci fan lines are drawn in the same fashion as speedlines, except that they are drawn at 38% and 62% angles. They are used in the same way as speedlines — to provide support during downward corrections. When one line is broken, prices will usually fall to the next lower line.
quotes: Fibonacci fan lines are drawn in the same fashion as the speedline. Except that Fibonacci lines are drawn at 38% and 62% angles.

concepts.title: Gann Lines
concepts.body: Gann lines are trendlines drawn from prominent tops or bottoms at specific geometric angles. The most important Gann line is drawn at a 45-degree angle from a peak or trough. Steeper Gann lines can be drawn at 63⅓ and 75 degree angles during an uptrend, and flatter lines at 26⅔ and 15 degree angles. It is possible to draw as many as nine different Gann lines.
quotes: The most important Gann line is drawn at a 45 degree angle from a peak or trough.

concepts.title: Gann Lines — Controversy and Limitation
concepts.body: Gann lines are somewhat controversial. Even if one of them works, you cannot be sure in advance which one it will be. Some chartists question the validity of drawing geometric trendlines at all.
quotes: Even if one of them works, you can't be sure in advance which one it will be.

concepts.title: Internal Trendlines
concepts.body: Internal trendlines are variations of the trendline that don't rely on extreme highs or lows. Instead, they are drawn through the price action and connect as many internal peaks or troughs as possible. The problem with internal trendlines is that their drawing is very subjective, whereas traditional trendlines drawn along extreme highs and lows are more exact.
quotes: internal trendlines are drawn through the price action and connect as many internal peaks or troughs as possible.
The problem with internal trendlines is that their drawing is very subjective

concepts.title: Reversal Day — Definition
concepts.body: A reversal day takes place either at a top or a bottom. A top reversal day is the setting of a new high in an uptrend followed by a lower close on the same day — prices set a new high at some point during the day (usually at or near the opening) then weaken and close lower than the previous day's closing. A bottom reversal day is a new low during the day followed by a higher close.
