concepts.body: The Arms Index (TRIN) measures the relationship between advancing/declining issues and their associated volume to gauge market sentiment. It can be smoothed for trend analysis, and the Open Arms variation provides additional interpretive flexibility.

concepts.title: McClellan Oscillator and Summation Index
concepts.body: The McClellan Oscillator is a breadth indicator derived from the difference between two exponential moving averages of advancing minus declining issues. The McClellan Summation Index is the cumulative total of the oscillator values and provides longer-term breadth signals.

concepts.title: New High-New Low Index
concepts.body: The New High-New Low Index tracks the number of stocks making new 52-week highs versus new 52-week lows to measure underlying market strength or weakness. It is a key stock market breadth indicator.

concepts.title: Upside vs. Downside Volume
concepts.body: Comparing upside volume (volume in advancing stocks) to downside volume (volume in declining stocks) provides insight into the internal strength or weakness of a market move. It is one of several stock market breadth indicators.

concepts.title: On-Balance Volume (OBV)
concepts.body: On-balance volume (OBV) is a cumulative volume indicator that adds volume on up days and subtracts volume on down days to measure buying and selling pressure. It is used to confirm price trends and identify divergences.

concepts.title: Volume as Secondary Indicator
concepts.body: Volume is considered a secondary indicator to price, used to confirm the strength of price moves and patterns. General rules include volume expanding in the direction of the trend and contracting during corrections.

concepts.title: Volume and Price Patterns
concepts.body: Volume provides confirmation in price patterns; expanding volume on breakouts validates the pattern while low volume breakouts are suspect. The degree of penetration and volume behavior together determine the significance of a pattern breakout.

concepts.title: Trendlines — Drawing and Using
concepts.body: Trendlines are drawn by connecting successive lows in an uptrend or successive highs in a downtrend. Their significance is determined by length, number of touches, and steepness. Small penetrations must be handled carefully, and price filters (such as the 3% rule or 2-day rule) help confirm valid breaks.

concepts.title: 3% Penetration Criterion
concepts.body: A trendline or support/resistance level is considered broken only when price closes beyond it by at least 3%. This filter helps avoid false breakout signals and whipsaws.

concepts.title: 2-Day Rule for Trendline Breaks
concepts.body: The 2-day rule requires that price close beyond a trendline for two consecutive days before the break is considered valid. This time filter reduces false signals from brief penetrations.

concepts.title: Fan Principle
concepts.body: The fan principle involves drawing a series of successively steeper or shallower trendlines as each prior trendline is broken. The breaking of the third trendline in the fan is considered a signal of a major trend reversal.

concepts.title: Percentage Retracements
concepts.body: Percentage retracements measure how much of a prior move price corrects before resuming the trend. The most common retracement levels are one-third, one-half (50%), and two-thirds of the prior move, with the two-thirds level being a critical threshold.

concepts.title: Ascending Triangle
concepts.body: The ascending triangle is a bullish continuation pattern with a flat upper resistance line and rising lower trendline. It typically resolves with an upside breakout and is one of the right-angle triangle formations.

concepts.title: Descending Triangle
concepts.body: The descending triangle is a bearish continuation pattern with a flat lower support line and declining upper trendline. It typically resolves with a downside breakout and is the mirror image of the ascending triangle.

concepts.title: Broadening Formation
concepts.body: The broadening formation is characterized by expanding price swings with diverging trendlines, the opposite of a symmetrical triangle. It often signals market instability and is typically a reversal pattern.

concepts.title: Flags and Pennants
concepts.body: Flags and pennants are short-term continuation patterns that form after sharp, nearly vertical price moves. They represent brief consolidations before the trend resumes, and the measured move technique is used to project price targets.

concepts.title: Wedge Formation
concepts.body: A wedge formation is characterized by converging trendlines that both slope in the same direction, either up or down. A rising wedge is typically bearish and a falling wedge is typically bullish.

concepts.title: Triangles and Elliott Wave Theory
concepts.body: Triangles in Elliott Wave Theory appear in wave four positions and as wave B of corrective patterns. Symmetrical, ascending, and descending triangles all have specific roles within the Elliott Wave framework.

concepts.title: 3-Box Reversal Point and Figure Chart
concepts.body: The 3-box reversal point and figure chart requires a price move of three boxes in the opposite direction to record a reversal column. Chart patterns, trendlines, and measuring techniques are all applied to this chart type for trading signals.

concepts.title: Point and Figure Trading Tactics
concepts.body: Point and figure charts are used to generate specific trading tactics including entry signals, price targets via measuring techniques, and trendline analysis. They are particularly useful for identifying support, resistance, and breakout levels.

concepts.title: Trading System Building
concepts.body: Building a trading system involves concept design, objective rules, testing code, evaluating results, and incorporating money management. Trading signals should be checked on a computer, and the system must be evaluated for robustness before live trading.

concepts.title: Trading Orders — Types
concepts.body: Key order types include market orders, limit orders (buy/sell), stop orders (buy/sell), and stop-limit orders. Each order type serves a specific purpose in trade execution, risk management, and position entry/exit.

concepts.title: Trending vs. Trading Markets
concepts.body: Markets alternate between trending and trading (range-bound) conditions, requiring different analytical approaches and tools. Identifying whether a market is trending or trading is essential for selecting appropriate technical strategies.

concepts.title: Trend Day
concepts.body: A trend day is a session in which price moves strongly in one direction from open to close with little retracement, as defined in Market Profile analysis. Recognizing trend days early allows traders to hold positions through the session.

concepts.title: Value Area in Market Profile
concepts.body: The Value Area in Market Profile represents the price range where approximately 70% of trading activity occurred during a session. It is used to identify fair value and potential support/resistance for subsequent sessions.

concepts.title: Time Price Opportunity (TPO)
concepts.body: Time Price Opportunity (TPO) is the basic building block of Market Profile, representing each 30-minute period at a given price level. Accumulating TPOs builds the Market Profile distribution used to identify value areas.

concepts.title: Technical Checklist
concepts.body: A technical checklist is a systematic review of multiple technical factors before making a trading decision. It ensures that traders consider all relevant indicators, patterns, and conditions before entering or exiting a trade.

concepts.title: Coordinating Technical and Fundamental Analysis
concepts.body: Technical analysis should be coordinated with fundamental analysis rather than used in isolation. Technical tools are used for timing entries and exits while fundamental analysis provides the underlying rationale for the trade direction.

concepts.title: Number Three in Technical Analysis
concepts.body: The number three appears repeatedly in technical analysis: three types of trends, three phases of major trends, three parts of head and shoulders patterns, three-box reversals, and the fan principle requiring three trendline breaks. This recurring significance makes three a key number in technical methodology.

concepts.title: Semilog Chart Scaling
concepts.body: Semilog (ratio) chart scaling plots equal percentage changes as equal vertical distances, making it more appropriate than arithmetic scaling for long-term charts or markets with large price ranges. It provides a more accurate visual representation of proportional price moves.

concepts.title: Internal Trendlines
concepts.body: Internal trendlines are drawn through the interior of price action rather than connecting extreme highs and lows, capturing the central tendency of price movement. They are related to Gann fan lines and Fibonacci fan lines.

concepts.title: Maximum Entropy Spectral Analysis (MESA)
concepts.body: Maximum Entropy Spectral Analysis (MESA) is an advanced mathematical technique used to identify dominant cycles in price data. It provides more precise cycle identification than traditional methods and is used in conjunction with other technical tools.

concepts.title: Equivolume and CandlePower Charting
concepts.body: Equivolume charting incorporates volume directly into the price bar by making bar width proportional to volume, while CandlePower combines candlestick charting with volume analysis. Both methods integrate price and volume into a single visual representation.

concepts.title: Starc Bands
concepts.body: Starc bands are volatility-based envelope bands developed by Manning Stoller, placed above and below a moving average using the Average True Range rather than standard deviation. They define high and low risk zones for trading.

concepts.title: Triple Crossover Method (Moving Averages)
concepts.body: The triple crossover method uses three moving averages of different lengths to generate trading signals. Buy signals occur when the shorter averages cross above the longer average, and sell signals occur on the reverse crossover.

concepts.title: Rising Three Methods Candle Pattern
concepts.body: The Rising Three Methods is a bullish continuation candlestick pattern consisting of a long white candle followed by three small declining candles contained within the first candle's range, then another long white candle closing above the first. It signals continuation of the uptrend.

concepts.title: Spinning Tops Candle Pattern
concepts.body: Spinning Tops are candlestick patterns with small real bodies and long upper and lower shadows, indicating indecision in the market. They signal that neither buyers nor sellers are in control and often precede reversals.

concepts.title: Shooting Star Candle Pattern
concepts.body: The Shooting Star is a bearish reversal candlestick pattern with a small real body at the lower end of the trading range and a long upper shadow. It signals that buyers pushed prices higher during the session but sellers regained control by the close.

concepts.title: Three Black Crows Candle Pattern
concepts.body: Three Black Crows is a bearish reversal candlestick pattern consisting of three consecutive long black (bearish) candles, each opening within the prior candle's body and closing near its low. It signals strong selling pressure and a potential trend reversal.

concepts.title: Three White Soldiers Candle Pattern
concepts.body: Three White Soldiers is a bullish reversal candlestick pattern consisting of three consecutive long white (bullish) candles, each opening within the prior candle's body and closing near its high. It signals strong buying pressure and a potential trend reversal.

key_quotes.text: Technical analysis is the study of market action, primarily through the use of charts, for the purpose of forecasting future price trends.
key_quotes.context: Murphy's formal definition of technical analysis, opening Chapter 1 on the Philosophy of Technical Analysis.

key_quotes.text: The author's strong belief is that a full appreciation of the technical approach must begin with a clear understanding of what technical analysis claims to be able to do and, maybe even more importantly, the philosophy or rationale on which it bases those claims.
key_quotes.context: Murphy explaining the importance of understanding the philosophical foundation before studying technical tools.

key_quotes.text: Since the principles of technical analysis are universal, it wasn't that difficult to broaden the focus to include all financial markets.
key_quotes.context: Murphy in the Introduction explaining why the book was expanded from futures to all financial markets.

key_quotes.text: It has been referred to by many in the field as the 'Bible' of technical analysis.
key_quotes.context: Murphy describing the reception of the original Technical Analysis of the Futures Markets (1986).

key_quotes.text: Market action discounts everything.
key_quotes.context: Stated as the cornerstone first premise of technical analysis.

key_quotes.text: The technician believes that anything that can possibly affect the price—fundamentally, politically, psychologically, or otherwise—is actually reflected in the price of that market.
key_quotes.context: Explanation of the first premise, market action discounts everything.

key_quotes.text: The whole purpose of charting the price action of a market is to identify trends in early stages of their development for the purpose of trading in the direction of those trends.
key_quotes.context: Explanation of the second premise, prices move in trends, and its practical implication.

key_quotes.text: The chartist does not necessarily try to outsmart or outguess the market.
key_quotes.context: Describing the technician's approach of letting the market indicate direction.

key_quotes.text: The chartist knows there are reasons why markets go up or down. He or she just doesn't believe that knowing what those reasons are is necessary in the forecasting process.
key_quotes.context: Distinguishing the technical approach from fundamental analysis.

key_quotes.text: The charts do not in themselves cause markets to move up or down. They simply reflect the bullish or bearish psychology of the marketplace.
key_quotes.context: Clarifying the role of charts as reflective tools, not causal agents.

key_quotes.text: Unless one accepts the premise that markets do in fact trend, there's no point in reading any further.
key_quotes.context: Emphasizing that accepting the trend premise is foundational to the entire technical approach.

key_quotes.text: the idea that markets are random is totally rejected by the technical community. If the markets were truly random, no forecasting technique would work.
key_quotes.context: Author refuting the Random Walk Theory from the technical analyst's perspective.

key_quotes.text: Far from disproving the validity of the technical approach, the efficient market hypothesis is very close to the technical premise that markets discount everything.
key_quotes.context: Author drawing a parallel between EMH and the technical premise that price discounts all information.

key_quotes.text: the academics have very eloquently stated the need for closely monitoring price action and the futility of trying to profit from fundamental information, at least over the short term.
key_quotes.context: Author turning the academics' own EMH argument into a case for technical price monitoring.

key_quotes.text: any process appears random and unpredictable to those who do not understand the rules under which that process operates.
key_quotes.context: Author using the analogy of an electrocardiogram to argue that market randomness is an illusion born of ignorance.

key_quotes.text: The illusion of randomness gradually disappears as the skill in chart reading improves.
key_quotes.context: Author encouraging readers that chart-reading skill dissolves the apparent randomness of markets.

key_quotes.text: Behavioral Finance which maintains that human psychology and securities pricing are intertwined. That, of course, is the primary basis of technical analysis.
key_quotes.context: Author noting that academic Behavioral Finance aligns with the foundational premise of technical analysis.

key_quotes.text: The technical principles that are discussed in this book can be applied universally to all markets—even mutual funds.
key_quotes.context: Author asserting the universal applicability of technical analysis tools across all asset classes.

key_quotes.text: Dow Theory still forms the cornerstone of the study of technical analysis, even in the face of today's sophisticated computer technology, and the proliferation of newer and supposedly better technical indicators.
key_quotes.context: Author introducing the Dow Theory chapter and affirming its enduring foundational importance.

key_quotes.text: The sum and tendency of the transactions of the Stock Exchange represent the sum of all Wall Street's knowledge of the past, immediate and remote, applied to the discounting of the future. There is no need to add to the averages, as some statisticians do, elaborate compilations of commodity price index numbers, bank clearings, fluctuations in exchange, volume of domestic and foreign trades or anything else. Wall Street considers all these things.
key_quotes.context: Hamilton articulating Dow's first tenet — that the averages discount everything.

key_quotes.text: Records of trading show that in many cases when a stock reaches top it will have a moderate decline and then go back again to near the highest figures. If after such a move, the price again recedes, it is liable to decline some distance.
key_quotes.context: Dow describing the action-reaction principle applied to market price behavior.

key_quotes.text: The most difficult task for a Dow theorist, or any trend-follower for that matter, is being able to distinguish between a normal secondary correction in an existing trend and the first leg of a new trend in the opposite direction.
key_quotes.context: Murphy describing the central practical challenge of applying Dow Theory's sixth tenet.

key_quotes.text: Prices plotted on ratio or log scales show equal distances for similar percentage moves.
key_quotes.context: Explaining the difference between arithmetic and logarithmic price scales.

key_quotes.text: Notice that the three year up trendline worked better on the log chart.
key_quotes.context: Caption note on Intel chart comparing arithmetic vs. log scaling for trendline analysis.

key_quotes.text: Open interest is the total number of outstanding futures contracts that are held by market participants at the end of the day. Open interest is the number of outstanding contracts held by the longs or the shorts, not the total of both.
key_quotes.context: Defining open interest and clarifying that it is not double-counted.

key_quotes.text: Futures chart services, along with most futures technicians, use only the total volume and open interest figures.
key_quotes.context: Explaining why aggregate rather than individual contract figures are used for forecasting.

key_quotes.text: Volume represents the total amount of trading activity in that market for that day. It is the total number of futures contracts traded during the day or the number of common stock shares that change hands on a given day in the stock market.
key_quotes.context: Defining volume as it applies to both futures and stock markets.

key_quotes.text: Chart analysis is actually a study of human psychology and the reactions of traders to changing market conditions.
key_quotes.context: The author explains the foundational reason why chart patterns and support/resistance levels work.

key_quotes.text: A support level, penetrated by a significant margin, becomes a resistance level and vice versa.
key_quotes.context: The core principle of support/resistance role reversal is stated.

key_quotes.text: There are sound psychological reasons why support and resistance levels can be identified on price charts and why they can be used to help predict market movements.
key_quotes.context: The author justifies the predictive utility of support and resistance through psychology.

key_quotes.text: support and resistance levels reverse roles only after a significant penetration. But what constitutes significant? There is quite a bit of subjectivity involved here in determining whether a penetration is significant or not.
key_quotes.context: The author acknowledges the subjective limitation in applying the role-reversal rule.

key_quotes.text: All of the previous selling near the top of wave 1 (creating the resistance level) has now become buying under the market.
key_quotes.context: The author explains the psychological mechanism behind resistance turning into support after a breakout.

key_quotes: There is a tendency for round numbers to stop advances or declines.
As a general rule, avoid placing protective stops at obvious round numbers.
protective stops on long positions should be placed below round numbers and on short positions, above such numbers.
two points are needed to draw the trendline, and a third point to make it a valid trendline.
Very often, the breaking of the trendline is one of the best early warnings of a change in trend.
The answer to that question is twofold—the longer it has been intact and the number of times it has been tested.
As a general rule, a close beyond the trendline is more significant than just an intraday penetration.
If the filter is too small, it won't be very useful in reducing the impact of whipsaws. If it's too big, then much of the initial move will be missed before a valid signal is given.
It's important to remember, however, that support and resistance areas only reverse roles when the market moves far enough away to convince the market participants that they have made a mistake.
once a trend assumes a certain slope or rate of speed, as identified by the trendline, it will usually maintain the same slope.
to have a valid breaking of a trendline, prices must close beyond the trendline for two successive days
once a trendline is broken, prices will usually move a distance beyond the trendline equal to the vertical distance that prices achieved on the other side of the line, prior to the trend reversal.
the breaking of the third line is the valid trend reversal signal.
the number three plays a very prominent role throughout the entire field of technical analysis.
most important up trendlines tend to approximate an average slope of 45 degrees
where steeper trendlines become necessary, it is best to resort to another tool—the moving average—which is the same as a curvilinear trendline.
It's surprising how often old trendlines act as support and resistance lines again in the future, but in the opposite role.
The basic up trendline can be used for the initiation of new long positions. The channel line can be used for short term profit taking.
trading in the opposite direction of the prevailing trend can be a dangerous and usually costly tactic.
the longer the channel remains intact and the more often it is successfully tested, the more important and reliable it becomes.

The breaking of the major trendline indicates an important change in trend. But the breaking of a rising channel line has exactly the opposite meaning, and signals an acceleration of the existing trend.

the failure of any move within an established price channel to reach one side of the channel usually indicates that the trend is shifting, and increases the likelihood that the other side of the channel will be broken.
Some traders view the clearing of the upper line in an uptrend as a reason to add to long positions.
Once a breakout occurs from an existing price channel, prices usually travel a distance equal to the width of the channel.
the basic trendline is by far the more important and the more reliable. The channel line is a secondary use of the trendline technique.
These countertrend moves tend to fall into certain predictable percentage parameters.
If the prior trend is to be maintained, the correction must stop at the two-thirds point.
If prices move beyond the two-thirds point, the odds then favor a trend reversal rather than just a retracement.
I prefer to combine both approaches for a minimum retracement zone of 33-38% and a maximum zone of 62-66%.
speed resistance lines (or speedlines) measure the rate of ascent or descent of a trend (in other words, its speed).
As with all trendlines, speedlines reverse roles once they are broken.
Even if one of them works, you can't be sure in advance which one it will be.
The wider the range for the day and the heavier the volume, the more significant is the signal for a possible near term trend reversal.
A top reversal day is the setting of a new high in an uptrend, followed by a lower close on the same day.
The selling climax is one of the more dramatic examples of the reversal day and, while it may not mark the final bottom of a falling market, it usually signals that a significant low has been seen.
One of the maxims often heard is that 'gaps are always filled.' This is simply not true. Some should be filled and others shouldn't.
As a rule, the heavier the volume after such a gap appears, the less likely it is to be filled.
Upside gaps usually act as support areas on subsequent market corrections.
In all cases a close below an upward gap is a sign of weakness.
When prices close under that last gap, it is usually a dead giveaway that the exhaustion gap has made its appearance.
The exhaustion gap to the upside followed by a breakaway gap to the downside completes the island reversal pattern and usually indicates a trend reversal of some magnitude.
The fact is that important changes in trend usually require a period of transition.
In times of doubt (and there are lots of those), a study of the volume pattern accompanying the price data can be the deciding factor as to whether or not the pattern can be trusted.
Price patterns are pictures or formations, which appear on price charts of stocks or commodities, that can be classified into different categories, and that have predictive value.
A market must obviously have something to reverse.
The first sign of an impending trend reversal is often the breaking of an important trendline.
The violation of a major trendline does not necessarily signal a trend reversal. What is being signaled is a change in trend.

The greater the size of the pattern—that is, the wider the price swings within the pattern (the volatility) and the longer it takes to build—the more important the pattern becomes and the greater the potential for the ensuing price move.
Topping patterns are usually shorter in duration and are more volatile than bottoms.
It is usually easier and less costly to identify and trade bottoms than to catch market tops.
Prices tend to decline faster than they go up.
At bottoms, however, the volume pickup is absolutely essential.
If the volume pattern does not show a significant increase during the upside price breakout, the entire price pattern should be questioned.
Everything in life is a tradeoff between reward and risk. The greater risks are compensated for by greater rewards and vice versa. Topping patterns are harder to catch, but are worth the effort.
The best known and most reliable of all major reversal patterns—the head and shoulders reversal.
The deciding factor in the resolution of the head and shoulders top is a decisive closing violation of that neckline.
It's important to remember that the objective arrived at is only a minimum target. Prices will often move well beyond the objective.
Reversal patterns can only be expected to reverse or retrace what has gone before them.

most price targets on bar charts are based on the height or volatility of the various patterns. The theme of measuring the height of the pattern and then projecting that distance from a breakout point will be constantly repeated.
The most skillful technical analysts are those who learn to blend all of those tools together properly.

despite all of these warnings, all that we know at this point is that the trend has shifted from up to sideways. This might be sufficient cause to liquidate long positions, but not necessarily enough to justify new short sales.

As a general rule, when a slight discrepancy exists between a projected price target and a clearcut support or resistance level, it's usually safe to adjust the price target to that support or resistance level.
Volume plays a much more critical role in the identification and completion of a head and shoulders bottom.
At the bottom, heavy volume is an absolutely essential ingredient in the completion of the basing pattern.
A market can fall just from inertia. Lack of demand or buying interest on the part of traders is often enough to push a market lower; but a market does not go up on inertia.
Prices only rise when demand exceeds supply and buyers are more aggressive than sellers.
One of the keys to survival in the financial markets is to keep trading losses small and to exit a losing trade as quickly as possible.
The ability and willingness to quickly recognize trading errors and to take defensive action immediately are qualities not to be taken lightly in the financial markets.
A single left shoulder usually indicates a single right shoulder. A double left shoulder increases the odds of a double right shoulder.
The top is often referred to as an 'M' and the bottom as a 'W.'
as is the case with all reversal patterns, the reversal is not complete until the previous support point at B is violated on a closing basis
The ideal top has two prominent peaks at about the same price level.

Volume tends to be heavier during the first peak and lighter on the second. A decisive close under the middle trough at point B on heavier volume completes the pattern and signals a reversal of trend to the downside.
The terms 'double top and bottom' are greatly overused in the financial markets.
Remember that, at a top, prices must actually violate the previous reaction low before the double top exists.
the final upside breakout would become a 'bull trap'
the upside breakout was on light volume and the subsequent decline on heavy volume—a negative chart combination
The analyst must face the realization that he or she is dealing with percentages and probabilities, and that there will be times when bad signals occur. That's simply a fact of trading life.
Because the technical odds usually favor continuation of the present trend, it is usually wise to await completion of the Pattern before taking action.
In general, most valid double tops or bottoms should have at least a month between the two peaks or troughs.
Spikes are the hardest market turns to deal with because the spike (or V pattern) happens very quickly with little or no transition period.
A daily or weekly reversal, on very heavy volume, is sometimes the only warning they give us.

These patterns usually indicate that the sideways price action on the chart is nothing more than a pause in the prevailing trend, and that the next move will be in the same direction as the trend that preceded the formation.
The treatment of all chart patterns deals of necessity with general tendencies as opposed to rigid rules. There are always exceptions.
As a general rule, prices should break out in the direction of the prior trend somewhere between two-thirds to three-quarters of the horizontal width of the triangle.
The actual trend signal is given by a closing penetration of one of the trendlines.
A minimum penetration criterion would be a closing price outside the trendline and not just an intraday penetration.
The triangle, therefore, provides an interesting combination of price and time.
Volume should diminish as the price swings narrow within the triangle.

The symmetrical triangle, by contrast, is inherently a neutral pattern.
The bullish breakout is signaled by a decisive closing above the flat upper trendline.
The triangle is considered an intermediate pattern, meaning that it usually takes longer than a month to form, but generally less than three months.
The volume tends to expand along with the wider price swings.
This situation represents a market that is out of control and unusually emotional.
Flags and pennants are among the most reliable of continuation patterns and only rarely produce a trend reversal.
They represent situations where a steep advance or decline has gotten ahead of itself, and where the market pauses briefly to 'catch its breath' before running off again in the same direction.
This is an unusually difficult pattern to trade and fortunately is relatively rare.
The pennant is identified by two converging trendlines and is more horizontal. It very closely resembles a small symmetrical triangle.
An important requirement is that volume should dry up noticeably while each of the patterns is forming.
Both patterns are relatively short term and should be completed within one to three weeks.
Pennants and flags in downtrends tend to take even less time to develop, and often last no longer than one or two weeks.
As usual, upside volume is more critically important than downside volume.

appear at about the halfway point of the move. In general, the move after the trend has resumed will duplicate the flagpole or the move just prior to the formation of the pattern.
a falling wedge is considered bullish and a rising wedge is bearish.

If the rallies are on heavier and the setbacks on lighter volume, then the formation is probably a continuation in the uptrend. If the heavier volume is on the downside, then it can be considered a warning of a possible trend reversal in the works.

The measured move, or the swing measurement as it is sometimes called, describes the phenomenon where a major market advance or decline is divided into two equal and parallel moves.
Both patterns take less time to develop in downtrends.
Confirmation refers to the comparison of all technical signals and indicators to ensure that most of those indicators are pointing in the same direction and are confirming one another.
Divergence is the opposite of confirmation and refers to a situation where different technical indicators fail to confirm one another.
divergence is a valuable concept in market analysis, and one of the best early warning signals of impending trend reversals.
Price is by far the most important. Volume and open interest are secondary in importance and are used primarily as confirming indicators.
Open interest represents the total number of outstanding longs or shorts in the market, not the sum of both.
if both participants in a trade are initiating a new position, the open interest will increase. If both are liquidating an old position, the open interest will decline.
volume should increase or expand in the direction of the existing price trend.
The level of volume measures the intensity or urgency behind the price move. Heavier volume reflects a higher degree of intensity or pressure.

If volume and open interest are both increasing, then the current price trend will probably continue in its present direction (either up or down). If, however, volume and open interest are declining, the action can be viewed as a warning that the current price trend may be nearing an end.
volume was mentioned several times as an important confirming indicator

Rising open interest in an uptrend is bullish.
Declining open interest in an uptrend is bearish.
Rising open interest in a downtrend is bearish.
Declining open interest in a downtrend is bullish.
Toward the end of major market moves, where open interest has been increasing throughout the price trend, a leveling off or decline in open interest is often an early warning of a change in trend.

sometimes the open interest dips slightly at the beginning of a new trend
Anyone who is not consulting these longer range charts is missing an enormous amount of valuable price information.
Long range price charts provide a perspective on the market trend that is impossible to achieve with the use of daily charts alone.
The purpose of weekly and monthly charts is to compress price action in such a way that the time horizon can be greatly expanded and much longer time periods can be studied.

The average trader's dependence on these daily charts, however, and the preoccupation with short term market behavior, cause many to overlook a very useful and rewarding area of price charting—the use of weekly and monthly charts for longer range trend analysis and forecasting.

The persistence of existing trends over long periods of time, in many cases for years, is a compelling argument against the claims of Random Walk Theorists that prices are serially independent and that past price action has no effect on future price action.
The proper order to follow in chart analysis is to begin with the long range and gradually work to the near term.
Long term charts are not meant for trading purposes.

Long term charts are useful in the analytical process to help determine the major trend and price objectives. They are not suitable, however, for the timing of entry and exit points and should not be used for that purpose.
The final point in this debate goes to the heart of the technical theory, which states that price action discounts everything, even inflation.
Signals on weekly charts become valuable filters for shorter term timing decisions.
Up trendlines on log charts are broken sooner than linear up trendlines.
There are those who claim that past price action has no bearing on the future. If you still believe that, go back and look at these long term charts again.
That's why it's a good idea to track weekly charts.
The moving average is a follower, not a leader. It never anticipates; it only reacts.
The moving average follows a market and tells us that a trend has begun, but only after the fact.
The longer averages work better as long as the trend remains in force, but a shorter average is better when the trend is in the process of reversing.

While two technicians may disagree as to whether a given price pattern is a triangle or a wedge, or whether the volume pattern favors the bull or bear side, moving average trend signals are precise and not open to debate.
a buy signal is produced when the shorter average crosses above the longer
a buy signal occurs when the 5 day average crosses above the 20, and a sell signal when the 5 day moves below the 20
the 10 day crossing above the 50 signals an uptrend, and a downtrend takes place with the 10 slipping under the 50
This technique of using two averages together lags the market a bit more than the use of a single average but produces fewer whipsaws

Using two standard deviations ensures that 95% of the price data will fall between the two trading bands.
In a strong uptrend, prices will usually fluctuate between the upper band and the 20 day average. In that case, a crossing below the 20 day average warns of a trend reversal to the downside.
Bollinger Bands work best when combined with overbought/oversold oscillators.
The centering technique is used almost exclusively by cyclic analysts to isolate underlying market cycles.
They trade in the direction of the trend. They let profits run and cut losses short.
They perform very poorly when markets get choppy and trade sideways for a period of time. And that might be a third to a half of the time.
it is dangerous to rely too heavily on the moving average technique

They stay in the market and get 'whipsawed' during trendless market periods.
a channel breakout system was his personal choice as the best starting point for all technical trading system testing and development

the four week rule performs at least as well as most other trend-following systems and better than many, but has the added benefit of incredible simplicity

Moving averages don't work all of the time. They do their best work when the market is in a trending phase. They're not very helpful during trendless periods when prices trade sideways.
The oscillator is only a secondary indicator in the sense that it must be subordinated to basic trend analysis.
Near the beginning of important moves, oscillator analysis isn't that helpful and can even be misleading. Toward the end of market moves, however, oscillators become extremely valuable.
The speed of Kaufman's 'adaptive moving average' automatically adjusts to the level of noise (or volatility) in a market.

Momentum measures the velocity of price changes as opposed to the actual price levels themselves.
If prices are rising and the momentum line is above the zero line and rising, this means the uptrend is accelerating.
When the momentum line begins to drop toward the zero line, the uptrend in prices is still in force, but at a decelerating rate. The uptrend is losing momentum.
Because of the way it is constructed, the momentum line is always a step ahead of the price movement.
Oscillator analysis should not be used as an excuse to trade against the prevailing market trend.
Buy positions should only be taken on crossings above the zero line if the market trend is up. Short positions should be taken on crossings below the zero line only if the price trend is down.
When the two moving average lines move too far apart, a market extreme is created calling for a pause in the trend.
In an uptrend, for example, the shorter line dips back to the longer average, but should bounce off it. This usually represents an ideal buying area.

the single most indicative characteristic of the Relative Strength Index

A trend day occurs when the longer term trader extends the range successively further. In this instance, the range is considerably more than double the initial balance with the longer term trader controlling direction as the market continues its search for a fair price.

A neutral day occurs when the longer term trader extends the range after the initial balance in one direction, then reverses and extends the range in the opposite direction. Neutral days indicate trader uncertainty and occur when the market probes or tests for price trend continuation or change.
Because the longer term trader is responsible for determining the market's directional movement, we monitor this activity to help detect evidence of a price trend.
Activity at the extremes provides the clearest indication of longer term trader influence, followed by range extension and then value area buying and selling.

The value area is an estimate of fair value which is approximated by one standard deviation of the session's trading volume.

Once balanced, a market moves to a state of imbalance which, more often than not, begins after a final test at the fairest price.
Every trend change occurs at a single moment in time, not conveniently at the end of the hour, day, week or month.
A market imbalance implies Price trend continuation, as the market works toward a new equilibrium.
Market balance, however, is fleeting and implies market change or a directional move (either up or down) is likely to occur.

summaries.summary: This chunk covers the front matter, table of contents, and opening philosophical framework of Murphy's Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets. It introduces the book's scope — applying technical analysis across futures, stocks, and other financial markets — and outlines the major topics covered, from Dow Theory and chart construction through Elliott Wave, cycles, and money management.

This chunk covers the table of contents, author biography, contributor bios, introduction to the revised edition, and the opening of Chapter 1 on the Philosophy of Technical Analysis. The most substantive trading content is the foundational definition of technical analysis as the study of market action through charts to forecast future price trends.

This chunk introduces the three foundational premises of technical analysis: market action discounts everything, prices move in trends, and history repeats itself. It explains the philosophical rationale behind the technical approach, particularly the 'discounting' premise and the centrality of trend identification to all technical methods.

This chunk addresses the Random Walk Theory and Efficient Market Hypothesis from a technical analyst's perspective, argues that markets only appear random to the untrained, introduces the universality of technical principles across all markets, and begins the chapter on Dow Theory with its historical origins.

This chunk covers the six basic tenets of Dow Theory as articulated by Charles Dow and later codified by Hamilton and Rhea. It explains how markets discount all information, how trends are classified into three timeframes and three phases, and how confirmation, volume, and trend continuation principles form the foundation of modern technical analysis.

This chunk covers the construction of daily bar charts, including how to plot price bars, opening and closing prices, and volume. It also introduces the concept of open interest in futures markets and explains the difference between arithmetic and logarithmic price scales.

This chunk explains the psychological foundations of chart patterns and support/resistance levels, then details the principle of role reversal — how support becomes resistance and vice versa after a significant penetration. It also raises the question of what constitutes a 'significant' penetration, acknowledging subjectivity in that determination.

This chunk covers the role of round numbers as psychological support and resistance levels, practical order placement rules around round numbers, and a thorough treatment of trendlines including how to draw, validate, use, and determine the significance of trendlines, as well as how to handle trendline penetrations and filter false breakouts.

This chunk covers trendline filters (price and time), the role-reversal principle of trendlines, measuring implications after a trendline break, the fan principle, the significance of the number three in technical analysis, trendline steepness and adjustment, and the channel line technique. It provides detailed rules for confirming trendline breaks and using trendlines for price objectives.

This chunk covers the practical application of channel lines in technical analysis, including how to use them for entries, exits, and trend assessment. It explains how breaking a channel line differs in meaning from breaking a basic trendline, and how failures to reach channel extremes signal weakening trends. It also describes how channels can be adjusted when prices move significantly beyond projected lines.

This chunk covers channel line measuring implications, percentage retracements (33%, 50%, 66%, 38%, 62%), speed resistance lines, Gann and Fibonacci fan lines, internal trendlines, and reversal days. It explains how countertrend moves fall into predictable percentage parameters and how various trendline tools can identify support and resistance levels.

This chunk covers reversal days (including selling climaxes), weekly/monthly reversals, price gaps (breakaway, runaway/measuring, and exhaustion), and the island reversal pattern. It then transitions into an introduction to price patterns, distinguishing between reversal and continuation patterns, and previews the major reversal patterns to be studied.

This chunk covers the general prerequisites and characteristics of major reversal patterns, including the need for a prior trend, the significance of trendline breaks, pattern size implications, differences between tops and bottoms, and the role of volume. It also introduces the head and shoulders reversal pattern as the most reliable major reversal formation.

This chunk covers the head and shoulders top pattern in detail, including its formation, volume characteristics, neckline break confirmation, return move behavior, and price objective measurement. It also introduces the inverse head and shoulders bottom as a mirror image of the top pattern.

This chunk covers the head and shoulders bottom pattern with emphasis on volume requirements, neckline slope variations, complex head and shoulders patterns, trading tactics, and failed patterns. It also introduces triple tops/bottoms and double tops/bottoms as related reversal patterns with their completion criteria and measuring techniques.

This chunk covers the double top and double bottom reversal patterns, including their formation, volume characteristics, measuring techniques, and variations from the ideal pattern. It also discusses filters to reduce false signals and warns about the overuse of the 'double top' label. False breakouts (bull traps) are introduced as a key risk when trading these patterns.

This chunk covers the completion criteria and size considerations for double tops/bottoms, introduces saucer and spike reversal patterns, and then transitions into continuation patterns — specifically triangles (symmetrical, ascending, descending) — detailing their construction, breakout rules, and time limits.

This chunk covers continuation patterns including symmetrical, ascending, and descending triangles, the broadening formation, and flags and pennants. It details volume behavior, measuring techniques, and forecasting implications for each pattern. Special cases such as ascending triangles as bottoms and descending triangles as tops are also addressed.

This chunk covers the characteristics and measuring implications of flags and pennants as continuation patterns. It details volume requirements, time duration, completion signals, and the 'half-mast' measuring technique for both patterns.

This chunk covers continuation patterns including flags, pennants, wedges, rectangles, the measured move, and introduces the continuation head and shoulders pattern. It details identification rules, volume characteristics, measuring techniques, and trading approaches for each pattern. Key emphasis is placed on the directional bias of wedges and the trading range behavior of rectangles.

This chunk covers continuation head and shoulders patterns, introduces the principles of confirmation and divergence, and then transitions into a detailed treatment of volume and open interest as secondary confirming indicators. It explains how open interest changes occur through different trade combinations and provides general rules for interpreting volume and open interest in relation to price trends.

This chunk introduces volume as a critical confirming indicator in technical analysis, referencing its role in major price patterns such as head and shoulders tops and double/triple tops. It emphasizes that volume behavior during pattern formation provides early warning signals of trend reversals. The text highlights the relationship between price movement direction and volume as a key analytical tool.

This chunk covers the principles of volume analysis including the concept that volume precedes price, the construction and interpretation of On Balance Volume (OBV), and the rules for interpreting open interest changes in futures markets. It also discusses OBV alternatives, limitations of volume analysis in futures, and specific open interest scenarios that signal bullish or bearish conditions.

This chunk summarizes the key rules governing volume and open interest in futures markets, including blowoffs and selling climaxes, the Commitments of Traders (COT) Report, and put/call ratios in options. It explains how open interest buildup during consolidations intensifies breakouts, how high open interest at tops is dangerous, and how commercial hedgers' positions can be used as a contrary indicator against small traders.

This chunk introduces the importance of long-term (weekly and monthly) charts for trend analysis, contrasting them with the more commonly used daily bar charts. It explains how futures technicians construct continuation charts to overcome the limited trading life of futures contracts. Several methods for building continuation charts are described, each with their own trade-offs.

This chunk covers the use of long-term weekly and monthly charts for market analysis, including the Perpetual Contract for continuous futures data, the argument against random walk theory based on persistent long-term trends, and the proper analytical sequence from long-term to short-term charts. It also addresses inflation adjustment of long-term charts and the distinction between long-term charts for forecasting versus daily/intraday charts for trade timing.

This chunk introduces moving averages as trend-following technical indicators, explaining their construction, types (simple, linearly weighted, exponentially smoothed), and basic signal generation. It covers the tradeoffs between shorter and longer averages in terms of sensitivity, lag, false signals, and trend-following effectiveness, concluding that using two averages is generally superior to one.

This chunk explains the double crossover method using two moving averages to generate buy and sell signals. It covers popular combinations such as the 5/20 day and 10/50 day averages, noting that this technique lags the market more than a single average but reduces whipsaws.

This chunk covers advanced moving average techniques including the triple crossover (4-9-18 day) method, moving average envelopes, and Bollinger Bands. It also explains the relationship between moving averages and market cycles, and discusses the statistical rationale for centering averages.

This chunk covers the use of Fibonacci numbers as moving averages, the application of moving averages to long-term charts, the pros and cons of moving averages, and introduces the Weekly Rule (4 Week Rule) developed by Richard Donchian as a highly effective trend-following mechanical system. It also discusses moving averages as oscillators and their application to other technical data.

This chunk covers the 4 Week Rule trading system, including modifications for non-continuous use, adjustments to time periods for sensitivity, and its relationship to market cycles and harmonics. It explains how the rule can be used as a standalone system or as a confirming filter alongside other technical indicators like moving averages.

This chunk covers the debate on optimization of moving average parameters, a summary of commonly used moving average combinations, the Adaptive Moving Average (AMA) concept by Perry Kaufman, and introduces oscillator analysis including momentum measurement, overbought/oversold conditions, divergence, and the three primary uses of oscillators.

This chunk covers momentum indicators, rate of change (ROC), dual moving average oscillators, the Commodity Channel Index (CCI), and introduces the Relative Strength Index (RSI). It explains how momentum measures acceleration/deceleration of price trends, how zero-line crossings generate signals, and how to identify overbought/oversold conditions using various oscillator tools.

This chunk discusses a problem identified by Wilder regarding the construction of momentum lines using price differences, specifically the erratic movement caused by sharp historical price changes being dropped off the calculation window.

This chunk covers the Relative Strength Index (RSI) formula, interpretation, and trading signals including overbought/oversold levels, failure swings, divergences, and trendline analysis. It also introduces the Stochastic oscillator (K%D), its formula, interpretation, and how to combine it with RSI for trading signals.

This chunk covers Larry Williams %R oscillator, the importance of trend in oscillator analysis, when oscillators are most and least useful, and provides a detailed explanation of MACD and the MACD Histogram including their construction, signals, and divergence analysis.

This chunk covers combining weekly and daily MACD signals for trend filtering, the MACD histogram's early warning capability, and the Principle of Contrary Opinion including Bullish Consensus numbers, overbought/oversold thresholds, and the strong versus weak hands concept in futures markets.

This chunk covers the use of Bullish Consensus numbers and Contrary Opinion in trading, including key thresholds, open interest considerations, and combining contrarian tools with technical analysis. It also introduces Point and Figure charting, contrasting it with bar charts and explaining its construction, flexibility, and signal generation.

This chunk covers point and figure chart construction, including how to vary box size and reversal criteria to adjust chart sensitivity for different trading timeframes. It explains intraday 1-box reversal chart construction with a Swiss franc futures example, and introduces the horizontal count method for deriving price objectives from congestion areas.

This chunk covers point and figure charting patterns including reversal patterns, fulcrum formations, and the construction and mechanics of the 3-box reversal point and figure chart. It explains how to plot X's and O's using only daily high and low prices, and details the rules for continuing columns and triggering reversals.

This chunk covers point and figure chart patterns including 8 buy signals and 8 sell signals, the drawing of 45-degree trendlines on 3-box reversal charts, horizontal and vertical measuring techniques for price objectives, and specific trading tactics for entry and exit using simple and complex formations.

This chunk covers point and figure chart trading tactics including entry variations, stop adjustment techniques, measuring methods (horizontal and vertical counts), and the advantages of P&F charting. It details how to use trendlines as filters, manage trailing stops, and handle prolonged moves without corrections (poles).

This chunk covers advanced point and figure charting techniques including logarithmic P&F charting with percentage-based box sizes, horizontal price count methods for projecting price objectives, and the application of moving averages to P&F charts. It references Thomas Dorsey's work on P&F charting and Kenneth Tower's computerized logarithmic P&F methodology.

This chunk introduces Japanese candlestick charting, covering the basic construction of candlestick charts using open, high, low, and close data, and explains fundamental candlestick types including Long Days, Short Days, Spinning Tops, and Doji variations. It then begins candle pattern analysis, covering reversal patterns such as Dark Cloud Cover, Piercing Line, Evening Star, and Morning Star, emphasizing that trend context is essential before applying any reversal pattern.

This chunk covers candlestick continuation patterns (especially Rising and Falling Three Methods), the use of computers for candle pattern identification, and the concept of filtered candle patterns using technical oscillators like Stochastics %D. It also provides a comprehensive library of bullish and bearish reversal and continuation candle patterns.

This chunk introduces the Elliott Wave Principle, covering its historical background, basic tenets including the five-wave impulse and three-wave corrective cycle, the concept of wave degrees, and the three main types of corrective waves: zig-zags, flats, and triangles. It also draws connections between Elliott Wave Theory and Dow Theory, and introduces the Fibonacci number sequence as the mathematical basis of the theory.

This chunk covers Elliott Wave corrective patterns including flat corrections (regular and irregular), triangles, the Rule of Alternation, and price channeling techniques. It explains how triangles function as continuation patterns within the Elliott Wave framework, how corrective waves tend to alternate in complexity, and how price channels are constructed to project price objectives and confirm wave counts.

This chunk covers Elliott Wave Theory's use of Fibonacci numbers as the mathematical basis for wave analysis, including Fibonacci ratios for price targets in impulse and corrective waves, percentage retracements, and time targets. It also discusses the differences between applying Elliott Wave to stocks versus commodities, and how combining wave form, ratio, and time analysis produces the highest-confidence signals.

This chunk summarizes the key elements of Elliott Wave Theory and transitions into a new chapter on Time Cycles. It covers the 17 core rules of Elliott Wave, its relationship to other technical tools, and introduces cyclic analysis concepts including cycle characteristics (amplitude, period, phase) and historical cycle research by Dewey and Hurst.

This chunk covers the fundamental principles of cycle theory in technical analysis, including the properties of price waves (amplitude, period, phase) and the six core cyclic principles: Summation, Harmonicity, Synchronicity, Proportionality, Variation, and Nominality. It explains how cycles can be identified, measured, and extrapolated to forecast future price peaks and troughs. A nominal model of harmonically related cycles affecting all markets is introduced.

This chunk covers time cycle theory in technical analysis, including the nominal cycle model, dominant cycle classifications, the Kondratieff Wave, the 28-day trading cycle, and the concept of left and right translation. It explains how cyclic principles underpin common charting techniques like trendlines, moving averages, and chart patterns, and provides rules for combining cycle lengths to determine trend direction and trading entries.

This chunk covers cycle translation (right/left), methods for isolating cycles using visual inspection and tools like the Ehrlich Cycle Forecaster, seasonal cycles across various markets, and stock market cycles including the January Barometer and Presidential Cycle. It provides specific seasonal tendencies for commodities, currencies, bonds, and equities.

This chunk covers the integration of cycles with technical indicators like moving averages and oscillators, introduces Maximum Entropy Spectral Analysis (MESA) for dynamic cycle detection, and transitions into a chapter on computers and trading systems, discussing charting software tools, data requirements, and how to organize and prioritize technical indicators.

This chunk covers Welles Wilder's Parabolic SAR and Directional Movement Index (DMI/ADX) systems, explaining how they work individually and in combination. It also discusses the pros and cons of mechanical trading systems and how system signals can be used as disciplining devices or screening alerts rather than purely mechanical triggers.

This chunk concludes a chapter on computers and trading systems, summarizing key points about Parabolic SAR, DMI/ADX, and mechanical trading systems. It then introduces the next chapter on Money Management and Trading Tactics, establishing that successful trading requires three elements: price forecasting, timing, and money management.

This chunk covers the three pillars of trading success: price forecasting, timing/tactics, and money management. It provides specific money management guidelines for futures traders including capital allocation percentages, diversification rules, and reward-to-risk ratios. It also discusses trading multiple positions split between trending and trading units, and how to handle periods of success and adversity.

This chunk covers trading tactics including breakout strategies, trendline breaks, support/resistance, percentage retracements, and price gaps for timing entries and exits. It also details how to combine technical factors with money management for stop placement, and explains the five main types of trading orders. Finally, it introduces the use of intraday charts for precision timing after the primary trading decision has been made.

This chunk covers intraday pivot point techniques for timing entries and exits, a comprehensive summary of money management and trading guidelines, asset allocation principles, and an introduction to intermarket analysis linking futures and stock markets. It also briefly introduces Market Profile as an innovative intraday trading approach.

This chunk introduces intermarket analysis, explaining the four-market linkage between currencies, commodities, bonds, and stocks. It covers program trading mechanics between S&P 500 futures and cash index, and explains how sector rotation within the stock market is driven by intermarket relationships.

This chunk covers intermarket relationships between the U.S. dollar and large/small cap stocks, the use of intermarket analysis for mutual fund investing, and the technique of relative strength analysis for comparing market sectors. It explains how to use ratio charts to determine which asset classes and sectors are outperforming, and how to rotate funds accordingly.

This chunk covers relative strength analysis for individual stock selection within market groups, the top-down market approach, and intermarket analysis including deflation scenarios and correlation measurement. It explains how intermarket relationships change under deflationary conditions and how correlation tools help traders weight intermarket signals.

This chunk covers the conclusion of intermarket analysis concepts and introduces Chapter 18 on stock market indicators, focusing on measuring market breadth. It explains the construction and interpretation of the advance-decline line, the use of breadth data such as advancing/declining issues and volume, and how comparing major market averages can reveal the true health of the overall market.

This chunk covers major stock market breadth indicators including the Advance-Decline line, McClellan Oscillator, McClellan Summation Index, New Highs vs New Lows, Upside vs Downside Volume, the Arms Index (TRIN), and the TICK indicator. Each indicator is explained in terms of construction, interpretation, and how divergences from major averages signal potential market turning points. The chapter emphasizes using multiple breadth measures together to confirm or warn of trend changes in the broader market.

This chunk covers the Arms Index (TRIN) and its smoothing methods, including the standard 10-day moving average and the Open Arms version, as well as Equivolume charting, a method that combines price and volume into a single rectangular bar chart.

This chunk covers Equivolume and CandlePower charting techniques that combine price and volume, methods for comparing market averages to gauge breadth and confirm trends, and a comprehensive technical analysis checklist for traders to use before and after forming market conclusions.

This chunk covers the conclusion of the book discussing the CMT designation, the MTA, IFTA, and the broad acceptance of technical analysis under various names including behavioral finance. It also introduces Appendix A on advanced technical indicators, specifically the Demand Index (DI) developed by James Sibbett, explaining its construction, interpretation, and divergence signals.

This chunk introduces the Herrick Payoff Index (HPI), an indicator developed by John Herrick that uses price, volume, and open interest to measure money flow into or out of commodity futures markets. It explains the basic interpretation of the HPI zero line crossings as buy and sell signals, and discusses the use of divergence analysis on weekly data for more reliable signals.

This chunk covers the Herrick Payoff Index (HPI) divergence signals, STARC Bands and Keltner Channels as ATR-based banding techniques with contrasting interpretations, the formula for the Demand Index, and an introduction to Market Profile as a statistical price distribution tool.

This chunk covers the Market Profile methodology developed by J. Peter Steidlmayer, explaining its graphic construction using Time Price Opportunities (TPOs), its organizing principles including auction dynamics, market balance/imbalance, and trader time frames, as well as the beginning of range development pattern classification.

This chunk covers Market Profile concepts including the four types of trading days (Normal, Normal Variation, Trend, and Neutral) and methods for tracking longer term trader activity through extremes, range extension, and value area analysis. It explains how longer term traders drive directional price movement and how their activity can be identified through profile graphics. Key tools include single print extremes, range extension monitoring, and TPO buying/selling counts.

This chunk covers Market Profile concepts including Range Extension, Value Area, and trend analysis using profile graphics, followed by an introduction to building a mechanical trading system including a 5-step plan and principles of good concept design.

This chunk covers the essentials of building a trading system, including a 5-step plan: choosing a trading idea, converting it to objective rules, visually checking it on charts, formally testing it with a computer, and evaluating results. It also discusses money management principles and diversification as critical components of profitable trading.

This chunk covers a trading system performance summary showing 36 years of results, followed by an appendix on continuous futures contracts including Nearest Contracts, Next Contracts, Gann Contracts, and Continuous Contracts. The performance summary demonstrates key metrics like profit factor and drawdown for a model system. The appendix explains how different types of futures contract derivatives are constructed for continuous data analysis.

This chunk covers the mechanics of Constant Forward Continuous Contracts (Perpetual Contracts) used in futures trading, followed by a glossary of key technical analysis terms including indicators, patterns, and analytical concepts from advance-decline line through MACD.

This chunk consists primarily of a glossary of technical analysis terms from Murphy's book, covering indicators, oscillators, price patterns, and market concepts. It defines key tools such as MACD histogram, McClellan oscillator, RSI, Stochastics, moving averages, and various price patterns with their trading signals and thresholds. The chunk also includes the beginning of the bibliography section.

This chunk consists entirely of a bibliography listing technical analysis books and authors, followed by a selected resources section listing financial book dealers and technical magazines. There is no trading instruction, methodology, or analytical content present. The material is purely reference and bibliographic in nature.

This chunk consists of a selected resources section listing technical software, market data providers, chart services, and technical organizations, followed by an index (A–H) of the book's key topics, concepts, indicators, and named contributors. No substantive trading instruction is provided in this section.

This chunk is an index section from Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets, covering entries from H through R. It references key concepts including intermarket analysis, moving averages, oscillators, candlestick patterns, point and figure charts, money management, and market profile, with page references throughout.

This chunk is the index section of Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets, listing topics from R through Z with page references. It covers a wide range of technical analysis concepts including reversal patterns, oscillators, trend tools, candlestick patterns, volume indicators, and trading system components. While primarily an index, it references numerous trading rules, indicators, and analytical frameworks discussed throughout the book.
